<b>Preface</b><br>
This textbook, Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools is written specifically<br>
for Form Five Students in the United Republic of Tanzania. It is written in accordance<br>
with the 2010 Advanced Mathematics Syllabus for Advanced Level Secondary<br>
Schools Education, Form V - VI issued by then, the Ministry of Education and<br>
Vocational Training.<br>
The book consists of ten chapters, namely; Calculating devices, Sets, Logic,<br>
Coordinate geometry 1, Functions, Algebra, Trigonometry, Linear programming,<br>
Differentiation, and Integration. Each chapter contains illustrations, activities,<br>
and exercises. The answer for questions for proofs, show, verifications, and some<br>
illustrations are not provided. You are encouraged to do all activities and exercises<br>
together with other assignments provided. Doing so, will enable you to develop<br>
the intended competencies.<br>
<b>Tanzania Institute of Education</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
1<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
<b>Scientific calculators</b><br>
<b>Sientific calculators are designed to</b><br>
compute numerical values in science,<br>
engineering, business, and mathematics.<br>
<b>They can handle various functions like</b><br>
polynomial, trigonometric, exponential,<br>
logarithmic, and many other functions.<br>
<b>There are two types of scientific</b><br>
calculators. These are programmable<br>
and non-programmable scientific<br>
calculators. Their differences are based<br>
on their functionalities. Programmable<br>
scientific calculators can write, store,<br>
and graph the input data, whereas non-<br>
programmable scientific calculators are<br>
not capable of graphing the input data. In<br>
this case, non-programmable scientific<br>
calculators will be emphasized as a part<br>
of calculating devices.<br>
<b>Activity 1.1: Identifying  different</b><br>
types of calculators<br>
<b>Individually or in a group, study</b><br>
carefully each of the following<br>
electronic calculators and perform<br>
the tasks that follow:<br>
(a) Simple calculator<br>
<b>Calculating devices</b><br>
<b>Introduction</b><br>
There are several calculating devices such as an abacus, slide rule, computer,<br>
electrical calculator, and electronic calculators. The useful devices for<br>
calculations are electronic calculators. Electronic calculators are made to<br>
serve different purposes like in businesses, banking, mathematics, science, and<br>
engineering. In this chapter, you will learn about scientific calculators and<br>
computer packages. Calculating devices are widely used in commercial and<br>
numerical computations. The competencies developed will help you to compute<br>
different calculations in mathematics, businesses, and other related fields.<br>
<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>One</b><br>
<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>One</b><br>

3.	 What challenges did you face in<br>
performing tasks 1 and 2?<br>
4.	 Share your results with your fellow<br>
students for more inputs.<br>

may differ depending on the manufacturers as<br>
well as on the models. However, their basic<br>
operations remain the same. Figure 1.1 shows<br>
the basic features of a non-programmable<br>
scientific calculator.<br>

(c)	Exponent and roots<br>
<b>A non-programmable scientific calculator</b><br>
can calculate the exponent of a number<br>
or the xth root of a number. The buttons<br>
for performing these two operations are<br>
labelled  ŌĆ£Ōł¦ŌĆØ and ŌĆ£ x<br>
ŌĆØ, respectively.<br>
<b>The following are steps for finding</b><br>
exponent of a number:<br>
Step 1: Enter the number.<br>
<b>Step 2: Press the ŌĆ£Ōł¦ŌĆØ key followed</b><br>
by exponent. If the exponent<br>
is in fraction, enclose it with<br>
parenthesis.<br>
<b>Step 3: Press the ŌĆ£ =ŌĆØ key to obtain</b><br>
the value of exponential.<br>
<b>The steps for finding the xth root of a</b><br>
number using a non-programmable<br>
scientific calculator are as follows:<br>
<b>Step 1: Enter the root index number</b><br>
(2 for square root, 3 for cube<br>
root, 4 for fourth root).<br>
<b>Steps of operating some basic keys</b><br>
of non-programmable scientific<br>
calculators<br>
<b>An electronic calculator with brand name</b><br>
<b>CASIO fx-991MS is a non-programmable</b><br>
scientific calculator which is used to perform<br>
different calculations. This calculator<br>
is common due to its affordability and<br>
availability in the market. Most of these<br>
calculators have multi-line displays, which<br>
enables the user to see both the expression<br>
under operations and the result.<br>
(a)	Replay (cursor control) button<br>
<b>A usefully non-programmable scientific</b><br>
calculators have a replay button which<br>
is used to review previous calculations.<br>
<b>The replay button is marked with four</b><br>
arrows as shown in Table 1.1. It is used<br>
for moving a cursor to the left, right,<br>
top, and bottom following the directions<br>
of the arrows. The ŌĆ£REPLAYŌĆØ button<br>
for some non-programmable scientific<br>
calculators has a functionality of<br>
ŌĆ£COPYŌĆØ as a second function.<br>

ŌĆ£SHIFTŌĆØ key and ŌĆ£ALPHAŌĆØ key. The<br>
ŌĆ£SHIFTŌĆØ key precedes all keys with<br>

Step 2: Press the ŌĆ£SHIFTŌĆØ key.<br>

ŌĆØ will be displayed.<br>
Step 4: Enter the number whose root is required.<br>
Step 5: Press the ŌĆ£ =ŌĆØ key.<br>

(d)	Memory keys<br>
A non-programmable scientific calculator can store answers for a while and retrieve<br>
using ŌĆ£RCLŌĆØ when needed for use in calculations.<br>
The following are the steps used to store results for a while in a non-programmable<br>
scientific calculator:<br>
Step 1: Type a number to be stored.<br>
Step 2: Press ŌĆ£SHIFTŌĆØ key.<br>
Step 3: Press ŌĆ£STOŌĆØ key and then ŌĆ£M+ŌĆØ to store the number displayed.<br>
Step 4: Press ŌĆ£RCLŌĆØ followed by ŌĆ£M+ŌĆØ key to recall the stored number.<br>
Step 5: Press ŌĆ£=ŌĆØ key to obtain the result.<br>

(f)	 Logarithmic functions to base<br>
10 and base e<br>
<b>Scientific calculators can compute</b><br>
common logarithms (base ten)<br>
and natural logarithms (base e) of<br>
numbers and their inverses.<br>
(i)	  Base 10 logarithms<br>
<b>The following steps are used to</b><br>
find the logarithm of a number<br>
to base ten:<br>
Step 1: Press the ŌĆ£logŌĆØ key.<br>
<b>Step 2: Enter the number</b><br>
whose logarithm is to<br>
be determined.<br>
<b>Step 3: Press the ŌĆ£ =ŌĆØ key,</b><br>
then the logarithm<br>
of a number will be<br>
displayed.<br>
<b>The following are steps for expressing</b><br>
numbers in scientific notation with<br>
specified number of significant figures<br>
or decimal places.<br>
<b>Step 1: Press the ŌĆ£MODEŌĆØ key five</b><br>
times.<br>
<b>Step 2: Select ŌĆ£FixŌĆ£ for writing figure</b><br>
in a certain number of decimal<br>
places, ŌĆ£SciŌĆØ for writing<br>
figure in a certain number<br>
of significant figures, and<br>
ŌĆ£NormŌĆØ for writing number in<br>
a normal form. The screen of<br>
the calculator looks as follows:<br>
<b>Step 3: Type the number of decimal</b><br>
places, or significant figures<br>
from the given limit 0 ~ 9.<br>
<b>Step 4: Use the number button to type</b><br>
the number of decimal places,<br>
or significant figures specified.<br>
<b>Step 5: Press ŌĆ£=ŌĆØ key to get the result</b><br>
in decimal places, or significant<br>
figures specified.<br>

<b>A number in degree format can be</b><br>
converted to radian format and vice<br>
versa by using a non-programmable<br>
scientific calculator. The conversion<br>
involves the relationship between radian<br>
and degree, that is; 2<br>
radian<br>
360 .<br>

=<br>

(i)	 Steps of converting a number<br>
from degree format to radian<br>
<b>Step 1: Change the calculator</b><br>
into radian by pressing<br>
the ŌĆ£MODEŌĆØ button<br>
four times and then<br>
press ŌĆ£ 2 ŌĆØ. The screen<br>
of the calculator looks<br>
as follows.<br>
<b>Step 2: Enter the required degree</b><br>
number to be converted to<br>
radians.<br>
<b>Step 3: Press the ŌĆ£SHIFTŌĆØ key</b><br>
followed by ŌĆ£ANSŌĆØ key.<br>
<b>Step 4: Press the ŌĆ£1ŌĆØ key to access</b><br>
degree symbol in the display.<br>
<b>Step 5: Press the ŌĆ£=ŌĆØ key to obtain the</b><br>
result in radian format.<br>
<b>Note that, convert minutes and seconds</b><br>
into degrees if the given angle contains<br>
them.<br>
<b>The following are steps for conversion</b><br>
of minutes and seconds into degrees:<br>
<b>Step 1: Enter the required angle to be</b><br>
converted.<br>
Step 2: Press the ŌĆ£=ŌĆØ key.<br>
<b>Step 3: Press the ŌĆ£SHIFTŌĆØ key followed</b><br>
by ŌĆ£ ,,,<br>
┬░<br>
ŌĆØ key.<br>
<b>Step 4: Press the ŌĆ£SHIFTŌĆØ key followed</b><br>
by ŌĆ£ANSŌĆØ key.<br>
<b>Step 5: Press the ŌĆ£1ŌĆØ key to access degree</b><br>
symbol in the display.<br>
<b>Step 6: Press the ŌĆ£=ŌĆØ key to obtain the</b><br>
result in radian format.<br>

(h)	Trigonometric functions<br>
<b>The values of trigonometric functions</b><br>
such as sine, cosine, tangent, and their<br>
inverses can be determined using<br>
scientific calculators. The buttons for<br>
these functions are written as ŌĆ£sinŌĆØ,<br>
ŌĆ£cosŌĆØ, and ŌĆ£tanŌĆØ on the calculator.<br>
<b>For a non-programmable scientific</b><br>
calculator, the steps for computing<br>
values of trigonometric ratios are as<br>
follows:<br>
<b>Step 1:  With the scientific calculator</b><br>
in degree mode ŌĆ£DŌĆØ, press<br>
the key of the trigonometric<br>
ratio required. For example,<br>
sin, cos, and tan.<br>
<b>Step 2: Enter  the angle whose</b><br>
trigonometric ratio is<br>
required.<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
15<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
Step 3:  Press the ŌĆ£=ŌĆØ key.<br>
<b>If the trigonometric function has an</b><br>
angle in radian format, for instance<br>
3 radians, or 3<br>
ŽĆ  radians, then the mode<br>
of the calculator should be in radians.<br>

<b>The following steps are used to convert</b><br>

key, 	that is ŌĆ£<br>

again, that is ŌĆ£       ŌĆØ key to<br>
obtain the result in decimal<br>
format.<br>

the result in decimal form.<br>
<b>Step 3: Press the fraction button key,</b><br>
that is ŌĆ£<br>

(j)	 Statistical calculations<br>
<b>In a non-programmable scientific</b><br>
calculator, statistical data in numerals<br>
can be processed to determine various<br>
statistical parameters such as sum, mean,<br>
and standard deviation for ungrouped<br>
and grouped data.<br>
(i)	  Ungrouped statistical data<br>
<b>Use the following steps in computations</b><br>
involving ungrouped statistical data:<br>
<b>Step 1: Press the ŌĆ£MODEŌĆØ key two</b><br>
times.<br>
<b>Step 2:  Select ŌĆ£SDŌĆØ mode by pressing the</b><br>
button numbered 1 as instructed<br>
on the screen of the calculator.<br>
<b>The screen of the calculator</b><br>
looks as follows.<br>
<b>Step 3: Type the number (value) then</b><br>
press the ŌĆ£M+ŌĆØ key.<br>
<b>Step 4: Repeat Step 3 for each of the</b><br>
values.<br>
<b>After entering the data into the calculator</b><br>
on the ŌĆ£SDŌĆØ mode, the steps to determine<br>
sum of data values  (<br>
)<br>
x<br>
Ōłæ<br>
, sum of squared<br>
values (<br>
)<br>
2 ,<br>
x<br>
mean ( ),<br>
x  and standard<br>
deviation(<br>
)<br>
x<br>
╬┤<br>
are as follows:<br>
Step 1: Press the ŌĆ£SHIFTŌĆØ button.<br>
<b>Step 2: Press the button key numbered</b><br>
1 or 2 to obtain different options<br>
of statistical values.<br>
<b>Step 3: Select the statistical value needed</b><br>
by pressing the relevant button<br>
as instructed on the screen of the<br>
calculator.<br>
<b>Step 4: Press the ŌĆ£=ŌĆØ key to obtain the</b><br>
result.<br>

(ii)	 Grouped statistical data<br>
<b>For the case of grouped data, apply the following steps:</b><br>
<b>Step 1:</b><br>
Press the ŌĆ£MODEŌĆØ key two times.<br>
Step 2:  	 Select ŌĆ£SDŌĆØ by pressing the key number 1.<br>
<b>Step 3:</b><br>
Type the class mark data.<br>
<b>Step 4:</b><br>
Press the ŌĆ£SHIFTŌĆØ key followed by comma to obtain ŌĆ£;ŌĆØ.<br>
<b>Step 5:</b><br>
Type the corresponding frequency of the class mark.<br>
<b>Step 6:</b><br>
Press ŌĆ£M+ŌĆØ key.<br>
Step 7:  	 Repeat steps 3 to 6 for each class mark and its corresponding<br>
frequency.<br>
<b>Step 8:</b><br>
Press the ŌĆ£SHIFTŌĆØ key, then press key number 1 or 2 to obtain<br>
different  options of statistical value.<br>
<b>Step 9:</b><br>
Select the statistical value needed by pressing the relevant key<br>
as instructed on the display of   the calculator.<br>
Step 10: Press the ŌĆ£=ŌĆØ key to obtain the result.<br>

Note that, once the statistical data are stored in a non-programmable scientific<br>
calculator, they cannot be cleared by turning off the calculator or by pressing the<br>
ŌĆ£ONŌĆØ or ŌĆ£ACŌĆØ keys. This can only be erased by either changing the ŌĆ£MODEŌĆØ<br>
or by running the steps for clearing statistical data.<br>
The following are steps for clearing statistical data in a non-programmable<br>
scientific calculator:<br>
Step 1: Press the ŌĆ£SHIFTŌĆØ key.<br>
Step 2: Press the ŌĆ£MODEŌĆØ key.<br>
Step 3: Press the ŌĆ£3ŌĆØ key.<br>
Step 4: Press the ŌĆ£=ŌĆØ key two times.<br>

(k)	Probability calculations<br>
A non-programmable scientific calculator can also be used to perform various<br>
probability calculations.<br>
(i)	  Combination of items<br>
To find the number of combinations of n items in groups of r  items by using a<br>
non-programmable scientific calculator, observe the following steps:<br>
Step 1: Type the n value.<br>
Step 2: Press the ŌĆ£SHIFTŌĆØ key.<br>
<b>Step 3: Press the ŌĆ£ ├ĘŌĆØ key to obtain n</b><br>
r<br>
C  function.<br>
Step 4: Type the r value to complete the expression for combination.<br>
Step 5: Press the ŌĆ£=ŌĆØ key to obtain the result.<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
23<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
(ii)	 Permutations of items<br>
To find the number of permutations of n items in groups of r  items by using<br>
a non-programmable scientific calculator, observe the following steps:<br>
Step 1: Type the n value.<br>
Step 2: Press the ŌĆ£SHIFTŌĆØ key.<br>
<b>Step 3: Press the ŌĆ£ ├Ś ŌĆØ key to obtain n</b><br>
rP  function.<br>
Step 4: Type the r value to complete the expression for permutations.<br>

(m)	 Calculations involving matrices<br>
<b>A non-programmable scientific calculator</b><br>
stores matrices and performs different<br>
operations such as addition, subtraction,<br>
and multiplication. In order to store the<br>
matrix in the calculator, some steps are<br>
required.<br>
<b>Steps for storing matrices in a non-</b><br>
programmable scientific calculator<br>
<b>Step 1: Press the ŌĆ£MODEŌĆØ key</b><br>
three times until the matrix<br>
mode ŌĆ£MATŌĆØ appears on<br>
the display and then choose<br>
it by pressing its option<br>
number.<br>
<b>Step 2:  While the calculator is in</b><br>
matrix mode press the<br>
ŌĆ£SHIFTŌĆØ key, then press<br>
the 4 key ŌĆ£MATŌĆØ.<br>
<b>Step 3: Press key number 1 and</b><br>
choose the name of the<br>
matrix, by pressing ŌĆ£ 1ŌĆØ for<br>
naming it as matrix A, ŌĆ£ 2ŌĆØ<br>
for B, and ŌĆ£3ŌĆØ for C.<br>
<b>Step 4: Set the dimension m n</b><br>
├Ś<br>
of the matrix by typing<br>
the value of m  (number<br>
of rows). Then press<br>
ŌĆ£ = ŌĆØ key and type again<br>
the dimension n  (number<br>
of columns) and then press<br>
ŌĆ£ =ŌĆØ key. If the matrix is 2<br>
by 2, then m  is 2 and n  is<br>
2 or if the matrix is 3 by 2,<br>
then m  is 3 and n  is 2.<br>
<b>Steps for inserting entries of a matrix</b><br>
<b>After the name and dimension of the</b><br>
matrix have been set, entries of the<br>
matrix are required. The following are<br>
steps of recalling the stored matrices:<br>
<b>Step 1: Press the first entry ŌĆ£</b><br>
11<br>
<b>MatA ŌĆØ if</b><br>
the matrix is named as A, then,<br>
press ŌĆ£ =ŌĆØ key.<br>
<b>Step 2: Press the second entry ŌĆ£</b><br>
12<br>
<b>MatA ŌĆØ</b><br>
then press ŌĆ£ =ŌĆØ key. Following<br>
the same steps until the last entry<br>
of the entire matrix is entered.<br>
<b>The entries of matrices are named as</b><br>
illustrated in the following cases:<br>

<b>Steps for editing entries of a matrix</b><br>

<b>Determinant, transpose, and inverse</b><br>
of a matrix<br>
<b>After inserting the entries of a matrix,</b><br>
the steps to obtain the determinant of<br>
the matrix are as follows:<br>
Step 1: Press "SHIFT" key.<br>
Step 2: Press 4, ŌĆ£MATŌĆØ key.<br>
<b>Step 3: Scroll the right arrow of</b><br>
the replay button and select<br>
ŌĆ£DetŌĆØ by pressing number<br>
1 key.<br>
Step 4: Again press "SHIFT"key.<br>
<b>Step 5: Press 4, ŌĆ£MATŌĆØ key, then</b><br>
select ŌĆ£MATŌĆØ by pressing<br>
number 3 key.<br>
<b>Step 6: Select the matrix whose</b><br>
determinant is required.<br>
<b>Step 7: Press the ŌĆ£=ŌĆØ key to obtain</b><br>
the result.<br>
<b>Note that, the same steps mentioned are</b><br>
used for finding transpose of the matrix<br>
by choosing transpose ŌĆ£TrnŌĆØ instead of<br>
ŌĆ£DetŌĆØ.<br>
<b>Inverse of a matrix</b><br>

<b>Step 3: Select ŌĆ£MATŌĆØ then press</b><br>
number 3 for the matrix.<br>
<b>Step 4: Select the matrix whose</b><br>

<b>Step 5: ŌĆ£MatA ŌĆØ will appear on the</b><br>

(n)	Evaluating derivatives and definite integrals of functions<br>
A non-programmable scientific calculator can be used to calculate the definite<br>
derivatives and integral of functions. The  key for integral of functions is labelled<br>
as dx<br>
Ōł½<br>
with its alternate functional key labelled as d<br>
dx . The functional key dx<br>
Ōł½<br>
is used for performing calculations which require integration, while<br>
d<br>
dx  is for<br>
differentiation.<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
30<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
<b>The following are steps for calculating</b><br>
definite integral by using a non-<br>
programmable scientific calculator:<br>
<b>Step 1: Press ŌĆ£ dx</b><br>
Ōł½<br>
ŌĆØ key.<br>
<b>Step 2: Press  the ŌĆ£ALPHAŌĆØ key in</b><br>
order to insert a function in<br>
terms of x. Every variable of<br>
the function to be inserted<br>
must be preceded by pressing<br>
ŌĆ£ALPHAŌĆØ key.<br>
<b>Step 3: Press comma, then insert</b><br>
lower limit of integration.<br>
<b>Step 4: Press again comma to insert</b><br>
upper limit of integration and<br>
close bracket.<br>
<b>Step 5: Press the ŌĆ£=ŌĆØ key to obtain</b><br>
the result.<br>
<b>Also, the following are the steps for</b><br>
calculating the derivative of a function at<br>
a point by a non-programmable scientific<br>
calculator.<br>
<b>Step 1: Press the ŌĆ£SHIFTŌĆØ button</b><br>
then press ŌĆ£ dx<br>
Ōł½<br>
ŌĆØ key.<br>
<b>Step 2: Press the ŌĆ£ALPHAŌĆØ key to</b><br>
insert a function in terms of<br>
x.<br>
<b>Step 3: Press comma, then insert</b><br>
a point given and close<br>
bracket.<br>
<b>Step 4: Press the ŌĆ£=ŌĆØ key to obtain</b><br>
the result.<br>

(o)	Conversion of units<br>
<b>Unit conversion is one of the functions of a</b><br>
scientific calculator. A non-programmable<br>
scientific calculators converts units under<br>
metric systems. Types of unit conversion to<br>
be carried out by a particular calculator are<br>
listed on the userŌĆÖs manual and on the inner<br>
part of the plastic cover of the calculator. Thus,<br>
before converting the units, identify the number<br>
which represent the type of conversion needed.<br>
The following are steps for unit conversions.<br>
Step 1: Press a number to be converted.<br>
Step 2:  Press the ŌĆ£SHIFTŌĆØ key.<br>
Step 3:  Press the ŌĆ£CONSTŌĆØ key.<br>
<b>Step 4:  Press a number to recall the</b><br>
unit.<br>
<b>Step 5: Press ŌĆ£=ŌĆØ key to obtain the</b><br>
result.<br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>

<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>

(p)	Physical constants and quick<br>
access to constants such as Pi and e<br>
<b>Physical constants are useful in</b><br>
calculations. Sometimes, constants<br>
are not given hoping that the learners<br>
can obviously get them from their<br>
scientific calculators. Constants which<br>
can be recalled by a particular scientific<br>
calculator are listed on the inner part of<br>
the plastic cover of the calculator as well<br>
as in the userŌĆÖs manual. As in the case<br>
of unitsŌĆÖ conversion, each constant is<br>
assigned a number in order to recall it.<br>
<b>For example, the constant for acceleration</b><br>
due to gravity (g) is assigned number 35.<br>
<b>Therefore, to recall it press the button</b><br>
ŌĆ£CONSTŌĆØ, then type 35 and press ( )<br>
=<br>
key to view its value.<br>
<b>Activity 1.11: Operation with constants</b><br>
<b>Individually or in a group, perform</b><br>
the following tasks by using a non-<br>
programmable scientific calculator:<br>
1.	 Identify any constants of your<br>
choice and their assigned numbers.<br>
2.	 Perform each of the following:<br>
(a)	Add any two constants.<br>
(b)	Subtract any two constants.<br>
(c)	Multiply any two constants.<br>
(d)	Divide any two constants.<br>
3.	 Identify all the steps you have<br>
used to obtain the values of the<br>
constants in task 2.<br>
4.	 Share the identified steps in task<br>
3 with your fellow students for<br>
more inputs.<br>
(q)	 Calculations involving vectors<br>
<b>A non-programmable scientific calculator</b><br>
stores vectors and performs different<br>
operations such as addition, subtraction<br>
and multiplication. In order to store a<br>
given vector in the calculator, some steps<br>
are required.<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
32<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
<b>For 2-dimension vector A , the entries are</b><br>
named as<br>
1<br>
2<br>
<b>VctA i</b><br>
<b>VctA j</b><br>
+<br>
.<br>
<b>For 3-dimension vector A , the entries are</b><br>

1<br>
2<br>
3<br>
<b>VctA i</b><br>
<b>VctA j</b><br>
<b>VctA k</b><br>
+<br>
+<br>
.<br>
<b>The following are steps for editing</b><br>
components of a vector<br>
<b>Step 1: Press ŌĆ£SHIFTŌĆØ key, then press</b><br>
the key number 5.<br>
<b>Step 2: Select ŌĆ£EditŌĆØ by pressing its</b><br>
option number.<br>
<b>Step 3: Select the name of the vector</b><br>
whose dimension has been set.<br>
<b>Step 4: Enter the correct value of the</b><br>
entry, then press ŌĆ£ = ŌĆØ key.<br>
<b>Continue with these steps until</b><br>
all the components are edited.<br>
(i)  Addition and subtraction of vectors<br>
<b>After inserting the components of a vector,</b><br>
the steps to add or subtract the given vector<br>
are as follows:<br>
Step 1: Press ŌĆ£SHIFTŌĆØ button.<br>
<b>Step 2: Press a key number 5, select the</b><br>
first vector by pressing a key<br>
numbered 3. Select the name<br>
of the vector to be added or<br>
subtracted by pressing its option<br>
numbers.<br>
<b>Step 3: Press ŌĆ£ + ŌĆØ key for addition or</b><br>
ŌĆ£ ŌłÆŌĆØ key for subtraction.<br>
<b>Step 4: Press a key numbered 5, then</b><br>
press a key numbered 3,  select<br>
the name of vector by pressing<br>
its option number.<br>
<b>Step 5: Press ŌĆ£ = ŌĆØ key to obtain the</b><br>
result.<br>
<b>The following are steps for storing</b><br>
vectors in a non-programmable scientific<br>
calculator<br>
<b>Step 1: Press the ŌĆ£MODEŌĆØ key three</b><br>
times until vector mode ŌĆ£VCTŌĆØ<br>
appears on the display and then<br>
choose it by pressing a key<br>
number 3.<br>
<b>Step 2: Press the ŌĆ£SHIFTŌĆØ button, and</b><br>
then press a key numbered 5.<br>
<b>Step 3: Press the key numbered 1 to</b><br>
set dimension.<br>
<b>Step 4: Press the key numbered 1 to</b><br>
choose the name of the vector,<br>
by pressing 1 for naming it as<br>
vector A, 2 for B, and 3 for C .<br>
<b>Step 5: Press the dimension m of</b><br>
the vector by typing the value<br>
of m . If the given vector is<br>
2-dimensional then the value<br>
of m  is 2 and m is 3 for a<br>
3-dimensional vector.<br>
<b>Steps for inserting components of a vector</b><br>
<b>Once the name and dimension of a vector</b><br>
have been set, the next step is to enter the<br>
entries of the given vector, which is done<br>
using the following steps:<br>
<b>Step 1: Press the first entry ŌĆ£</b><br>
1<br>
<b>VctA ŌĆØ</b><br>
if the vector is namedA . Then,<br>
press ŌĆ£ =ŌĆØ key.<br>
<b>Step 2: Press the second entry ŌĆ£</b><br>
2<br>
<b>VctA ŌĆØ,</b><br>
then press ŌĆ£=ŌĆØ key. If the vector<br>
is of a 3-dimension follow the<br>
same steps until the final entry<br>
is entered.<br>
<b>The components of vectors are named as</b><br>
illustrated in the following cases.<br>

(ii) Modulus of a vector<br>
<b>After entering the components of vector,</b><br>
use the following steps  to determine its<br>

<b>Steps 1: Press the ŌĆ£SHIFTŌĆØ key, then</b><br>
press ŌĆ£ ) ŌĆØ key to obtain ŌĆ£AbsŌĆØ.<br>
<b>Step 2: Press theŌĆ£SHIFTŌĆØ key, then</b><br>

<b>Step 4: Select the name of the vector</b><br>

(iii) Dot product of vectors<br>
<b>After entering the components of vectors,</b><br>
use the following steps to determine the<br>

<b>Step 1: Press the ŌĆ£SHIFTŌĆØ key, then</b><br>
press 5 ŌĆ£VCTŌĆØ key.<br>
<b>Step 2: Select ŌĆ£VctŌĆØ by pressing the</b><br>

<b>Step 3: Select the first vector to be</b><br>

<b>Step 4: Scroll the right arrow of replay</b><br>

Step 5: Again press the ŌĆ£SHIFTŌĆØ key.<br>
<b>Step 6: Press the key numbered 5, then</b><br>
select ŌĆ£VctŌĆØ by pressing the key<br>
numbered 3.<br>
Step 7: Select vectors to be dotted.<br>
<b>Step 8: Press ŌĆ£= ŌĆØ key to obtain the</b><br>
result.<br>
(iv) Cross product of vectors<br>

<b>Step 1: Press the ŌĆ£SHIFTŌĆØ key, then</b><br>
press a key numbered 5.<br>
<b>Step 2: Select ŌĆ£VctŌĆØ by pressing a key</b><br>
numbered 3.<br>
<b>Step 3: Select the first vector to be</b><br>
crossed.<br>
<b>Step 4: Press ŌĆ£├ŚŌĆØ key, then repeat</b><br>
<b>Step 1 to Step 2 and select the</b><br>
second vector to be crossed.<br>
<b>Follow the same steps until</b><br>
the last vector to be crossed<br>
is inserted.<br>
<b>Step 5: Press ŌĆ£ = ŌĆØ key to obtain the</b><br>
result.<br>

(r)	Calculations involving complex<br>
numbers<br>
<b>A complex number is a number in a</b><br>
form a<br>
bi<br>
+<br>
where a is the real part and<br>
b is an imaginary part of a complex<br>
number.  A non-programmable scientific<br>
calculator can perform different operations<br>
on complex numbers such as addition,<br>
subtraction, multiplication, and division.<br>
(i) Addition and subtraction of<br>
complex numbers<br>
<b>To add or subtract complex numbers using</b><br>
a non-programmable scientific calculator<br>
use the following steps:<br>
<b>Step 1: Press the ŌĆ£MODEŌĆØ key</b><br>
once, then select complex<br>
mode ŌĆ£CMPLXŌĆØ by pressing<br>
number 2 key.<br>
<b>Step 2: Insert open bracket by</b><br>
pressing ŌĆ£(ŌĆØ key, insert real<br>
part of the complex number<br>
followed by ŌĆ£+ŌĆØ or ŌĆ£ ŌłÆŌĆØ sign,<br>
then by imaginary part, press<br>
the ŌĆ£ENGŌĆØ key to  insert i<br>
then insert  close bracket by<br>
pressing ŌĆ£)ŌĆØ key.<br>
<b>Step 3: Press ŌĆ£+ŌĆØ key for addition or</b><br>
ŌĆ£ ŌłÆŌĆØ for subtraction.<br>
<b>Step 4: Repeat step 2 to insert the</b><br>
second complex to be added<br>
or subtracted.<br>
<b>Step 5: Press ŌĆ£=ŌĆØ key to obtain the</b><br>
real part of the result.<br>
<b>Step 6: Press the ŌĆ£SHIFTŌĆØ key,</b><br>
then ŌĆ£=ŌĆØ key to obtain the<br>
imaginary part of the result.<br>

ŌĆ£CMPLXŌĆØ by pressing a<br>

<b>Step 2:  Insert open bracket by pressing</b><br>
ŌĆ£(ŌĆØ key, insert a real part of<br>

by ŌĆ£+ŌĆØ or ŌĆ£ŌłÆŌĆØ sign, then by<br>

key to  insert i then insert<br>

ŌĆ£)ŌĆØ key.<br>
<b>Step 3:   Press ŌĆ£├ŚŌĆØ key for</b><br>

for division.<br>
<b>Step 4: Repeat step 2 to insert the</b><br>

<b>Step 5: Press ŌĆ£=ŌĆØ key to obtain the</b><br>

ŌĆ£=ŌĆØ key to obtain the imaginary<br>

ŌĆ£)ŌĆØ to obtain ŌĆ£AbsŌĆØ.<br>

<b>Step 4: Press ŌĆ£=ŌĆØ key to obtain the</b><br>

ŌĆ£(ŌĆØ  key to obtain ŌĆ£argŌĆØ key.<br>
<b>Step 3: Insert the complex number</b><br>

(s)  Errors in a non-programmable<br>
scientific calculator<br>
<b>A non-programmable scientific calculator</b><br>
displays an error message whenever an error<br>
is made. The replay button can be used to<br>
move the cursor within the calculation on<br>
the calculator screen, and then the character<br>
can be inserted at the location simply by<br>
pressing the appropriate buttons. In some<br>
cases it may be easier to abandon what has<br>
been typed and start again by pressing the all<br>
clear ŌĆ£ACŌĆØ key. The following are messages<br>
that a calculator displays to alert an error<br>
that has been occurred.<br>
(i) Math error<br>
<b>This occurs when the calculation inserted</b><br>
make sense but the result cannot be<br>
calculated, such as attempting to divide by<br>
zero or when the calculation is too large for<br>
the calculator to handle. For instance, 5<br>
0<br>
├Ę<br>
, calculator will display ŌĆ£Math ERRORŌĆØ,<br>
because a number cannot be divided by zero.<br>
(ii) Syntax error<br>
<b>This occurs when the format of the</b><br>
calculation inserted does not make sense.<br>
<b>For instance,</b><br>
45 tan,<br>
┬░<br>
calculator will display ŌĆ£syntax<br>
<b>ERRORŌĆØ since there is problem with the</b><br>
format of calculation inserted.<br>
(iii) Dimension error<br>
<b>This will occur especially when preforming</b><br>
calculation with matrices or vectors whose<br>
dimensions do not allow that type of<br>
calculations. For instance,<br>
1<br>
2<br>
0<br>
6<br>
2<br>
6<br>
0<br>
5 ,<br>
3<br>
2<br>
1<br>
3<br>
ŌÄøŌÄ×ŌÄø<br>
ŌÄ×<br>
ŌÄ£ŌÄ¤ŌÄ£<br>
ŌÄ¤<br>
ŌÄ£ŌÄ¤ŌÄ£<br>
ŌÄ¤<br>
ŌÄ£ŌÄ¤ŌÄ£<br>
ŌÄ¤<br>
ŌÄØŌÄĀŌÄØ<br>
ŌÄĀ<br>
the calculator will display ŌĆ£Dim ERRORŌĆØ<br>
since it is not possible to multiply a 3 1<br>
├Ś<br>
matrix by 3 3<br>
├Ś matrix.<br>
(iv) Stack error<br>
<b>This occurs when the capacity of numeric</b><br>
stack or command stack exceeded<br>
during calculation, for instance, find<br>
<b>T</b><br>

<b>Computer packages are used in various</b><br>
disciplines depending on the application<br>
relevant to the field. There are various<br>

computer packages which are useful in<br>

<b>The computerŌĆÖs calculator</b><br>
<b>The computerŌĆÖs calculator offers precise</b><br>
calculations and has a powerful interface. It<br>
has integrated the basic standard calculations<br>
with programming, scientific calculations,<br>
and statistics. Furthermore, there are also<br>
other features which are very useful like<br>
mortgage calculation and multifunctional<br>
converter.<br>
<b>Opening the computerŌĆÖs calculator</b><br>
<b>The following are useful steps to access the</b><br>
computerŌĆÖs calculator in Window 10<br>
(See Figure 1.2):<br>
Step 1: Click the Start Menu.<br>
<b>Step 2: Choose the calculator in the given</b><br>
options.<br>
<b>Figure 1.2: Start menu of the computer</b><br>
<b>ComputerŌĆÖs calculation modes</b><br>
<b>Even though Windows 10 has</b><br>
a different version as compared<br>
to Windows 7, the computer<br>
calculators are the same in both<br>
operating systems. The interfaces<br>
are the same and their features are<br>
identical.<br>
<b>The computer calculator in</b><br>
<b>Windows 10 has four (4) main</b><br>
modes for which calculations can<br>
be done:<br>
(a)	The standard mode<br>
(b)	The programmer mode<br>
(c)	The scientific mode<br>
(d)	The statistics mode<br>
<b>Each mode enables the user to</b><br>
perform basic operations, unit<br>
conversion, time conversion, or<br>
worksheet calculations. Thus, the<br>
mode and type of operations have<br>
to be set first before calculations<br>
so that the particular kind of<br>
functional keys can be seen and<br>
used.<br>
(a)	The standard mode<br>
<b>When the computerŌĆÖs calculator is</b><br>
opening, the standard mode will<br>
be selected by default as shown<br>
in Figure 1.3. The keyboard<br>
number values, the keypad<br>
(with the number key activated)<br>
or the mouse can be used to do<br>
calculations.<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
38<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
<b>Figure 1.3: Standard mode of the computerŌĆÖs</b><br>
calculator<br>
(b)	The programmer mode<br>
<b>This mode gives the function keys of a</b><br>
programmable scientific calculator. It<br>
offers the possibility to do operations with<br>
bases such as binary, octal, hexadecimal,<br>
and decimal. One can convert values<br>
from one base to another. For example,<br>
conversion from a base two number<br>
system (binary 0, 1) to a base ten number<br>
system (decimal 0 ŌĆō 9). This mode also<br>
offers the logical bit operations such as<br>
XOR, OR, and AND.<br>
<b>To access this mode, click on the option</b><br>
menu in the computerŌĆÖs calculator and<br>
select the programmer option. (See<br>
Figure.1.4).<br>
<b>Figure 1.4: The programmer mode of the</b><br>
computerŌĆÖs calculator<br>
(c)	The scientific mode<br>
<b>This mode resembles a non-programmable</b><br>
scientific calculator. In this mode, any<br>
calculation which can be performed using<br>
a scientific calculator may be executed.<br>
<b>To access this mode, click on the option</b><br>
menu in the computerŌĆÖs calculator and<br>
select the scientific option. (See Figure.<br>
1.5).<br>
<b>Figure 1.5: The scientific mode of the</b><br>
computerŌĆÖs calculator<br>

(d)	Statistics mode<br>
<b>Like in the standard mode of the</b><br>
computerŌĆÖs calculator, operations in<br>

a number, followed by an operation then<br>
insert the second number, and finish up<br>
by clicking the equal sign to obtain the<br>
final answer. (See Figure 1.6)<br>
<b>Figure. 1.6: Statistics mode of the</b><br>
computerŌĆÖs calculator<br>
<b>How to create a dataset in statistics</b><br>
mode<br>
<b>A dataset is a collection of data in</b><br>
numbers that can be used in performing<br>
different operations. To create a dataset,<br>
click the number (s) which form the set<br>
of a particular data and then click the add<br>
button. Repeat these steps until all the<br>
data are entered. Each time you press the<br>
add button, the number of count changes.<br>
ŌĆ£CountŌĆØ represents the total number of<br>
data entered. The data will be displayed<br>
on the dataset list. When all the data are<br>
entered in the dataset list, click on any<br>
operation such as sum, mean, or standard<br>
deviation and the corresponding result<br>
will be displayed at the bottom of the<br>
dataset list.<br>
<b>Note that, clicking the C button in the</b><br>
statistics mode deletes the current data<br>
entered before clicking the add button<br>
and the CAD button clears all the data<br>
from the dataset.<br>
<b>Other options in the computerŌĆÖs</b><br>
calculator<br>
<b>A computerŌĆÖs calculator includes other</b><br>
tools which are used in measurements<br>
such as mass, time, volume, weight,<br>
light intensity, and distance. Thus, a<br>
computerŌĆÖs calculator has more functions<br>
to do than a pocket or hand- scientific<br>
calculator.<br>

<b>Spreadsheets</b><br>
<b>A spreadsheet is an interactive computer</b><br>
application program for organizing,<br>
analyzing, and storing data. The program<br>
operates on data represented as cells of<br>
an array organized in rows and columns.<br>
<b>Each cell of the array may contain either</b><br>
numeric or text data.<br>
<b>The results from the formulae can</b><br>
automatically be calculated and displayed<br>
in the other cell within the worksheet.<br>
<b>Microsoft Excel is one of the spreadsheet</b><br>
programs offered in the Microsoft Office<br>
software package. This program allows<br>
the user to perform calculations such as<br>
average, addition, subtraction, and finding<br>
the maximum and minimum values of<br>
numbers. In addition, Microsoft Excel can<br>
be used to create histograms, pie charts,<br>
and plot graphs of any function.<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
41<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
<b>Starting Microsoft Excel</b><br>
The steps for opening Microsoft Excel depend on the type of a computerŌĆÖs operating<br>
system (Microsoft Windows). In this section, Microsoft Excel as one of the<br>
applications (Microsoft Offices 2016) will be used as an example. The following<br>
steps shows how to open Excel in Microsoft Windows 10:<br>
1.	 Click the start menu.<br>
2.	 Select the Microsoft Office.<br>
3.	 Select the Microsoft Excel.<br>
Then, the program opens in a display similar to that shown in Figure1.7.<br>
<b>Figure 1.7: Microsoft Excel spreadsheet display</b><br>
<b>The Excel sheet is divided into grids</b><br>
called cells where the data can be<br>
entered. The cell is named by notation<br>
representing column and row. The<br>
columns are assigned capital letters of<br>
the alphabet while the rows are assigned<br>
numbers. Thus, the first cell is named as<br>
<b>A1 which means the cell is in column</b><br>
<b>A and in row 1 as shown in Figure.1.7,</b><br>
while B1 means the cell is in another<br>
column B in the same row (row 1),<br>
and the fourth cell vertically below the<br>
<b>B1 cell is B4. Before performing any</b><br>
calculations, data (numbers) have to<br>
be entered to the cells. Normally, the<br>
cell name appears in the ŌĆ£Name boxŌĆÖ as<br>
shown in Figure 1.7.<br>
<b>To enter the data, click on the cell and</b><br>
type the data. The next data can be<br>
entered along the same column or row.<br>
<b>The vertical and horizontal navigation</b><br>
arrows are used for moving the cursor to<br>
another cell. You may also use the ŌĆ£mouse<br>
pointerŌĆØ by clicking the required cell.<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

<b>Activity 1.12: Entering and analyzing data in Excel</b><br>
<b>Individually or in a group, perform the following tasks:</b><br>
1.	 Use Microsoft Excel to enter the following scores in a worksheet; 30, 50,<br>
60, 70, 25, 80, 20, 50, 40, 90, 30, and 70.<br>
2.	 Sort the score in descending order.<br>
3.	 Find the sum, average, maximum, and minimum score.<br>
4.	 Identify the steps you have used to insert data, sort and analyse the data in<br>
tasks 1, 2, and 3, respectively.<br>
5.	 Share the identified steps with your fellows students.<br>
Note that, in Excel sheet, for easy operations, the data have to be entered in<br>
one column or one row. For example, the column of the data in Activity 1.12<br>
should appear as shown in Figure 1.8.<br>
<b>Figure 1.8: Scores of students in a certain test</b><br>
<b>Rearrangement of data</b><br>
To rearrange the data in Excel, first select the data, then on home tab, click the<br>
sort button and select the type of arrangement preferred. Observe the illustration<br>
in Figure.1.9.<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>

<b>Calculation of the sum and average of data</b><br>
To find the sum or average of data, select the data and then click the sum/average<br>
button. On the menu that appears after clicking, select the option either sum or<br>
average. The result will be displayed at the next cell from the last.  Figure.1.10<br>
illustrates the steps for calculating the sum and average of data.<br>
<b>Figure 1.10: Determination of the sum or average of data</b><br>
<b>Setting a formula for performing calculations in Excel</b><br>
In Excel, a formula can be set and applied to execute operations. In other words,<br>
Excel provides room for processing data using a formula of userŌĆÖs choice. Thus,<br>
the user may create a formula that involves some steps which depend on the type<br>
and length of the formula.<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
44<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>

should be aware of the basic symbols.<br>
<b>Table 1.2 shows basic symbols used in</b><br>
defining a formula.<br>
<b>Table 1.3: Basic calculation symbols</b><br>
in Excel<br>
<b>Symbol</b><br>
<b>Meaning</b><br>

<b>Excel formula for addition</b><br>
<b>The process of adding numbers in Excel</b><br>
starts with an equal sign( )<br>
= , followed<br>
by the first term/number, then plus sign<br>
( )<br>
+ , and then the second term and so on.<br>
<b>For example, to add a number in A1 and</b><br>
the other in B1, use the following steps:<br>
<b>Step 1: Click the cell where you want</b><br>
the result to appear.<br>
<b>Step 2: Put the equal sign( )</b><br>
= .<br>
<b>Step 3: Click the cell of the number</b><br>
before the addition sign.<br>
<b>Step 4: Type the addition sign( )</b><br>
+ .<br>
<b>Step 5: Click the cell of the number to</b><br>
be added.<br>
<b>Step 6: Press enter key to obtain the</b><br>
answer.<br>
<b>While following the addition steps, the</b><br>
formula is created and displayed along<br>
the fx area (formula bar) as illustrated<br>
in Figure 1.11.<br>
<b>Figure 1.11: Cell selection and formula display</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
45<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
The cell at which the formula is typed can be used as a reference. If the same<br>
formula applies to other data, place the cursor at the bottom of the right corner of<br>
the reference cell (the symbol + will appear), click, and hold the cursor and drag<br>
down across all the rows. After releasing the mouse, the formula will be applied<br>
to all the cells through which the dragging process took place. (See Figure 1.12).<br>
<b>Figure 1.12: Sum of A1 and B1</b><br>

<b>Chapter summary</b><br>
1.	 Scientific calculators are calculators designed to compute numerical values<br>
in science, engineering, business, and mathematics.<br>
2.	 Scientific calculators have many features that may differ depending on the<br>
manufacturers as well as the model.<br>
3.	 Most of the buttons of a scientific calculator have more than one function.<br>
4.	 A proposed electronic calculator named CASIO fx-991MS is a non-<br>
programmable scientific calculator which performs different calculations<br>
in mathematics.<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
50<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
5.	 A non-programmable scientific calculator displays an error message<br>
whenever an error is made.<br>
6.	 Computer packages are used in various disciplines depending in their<br>
applications in the intended field.<br>
7.	 A computerŌĆÖs calculator gives correct calculations and has a powerful<br>
interface.<br>
8.	 A spreadsheet is an interactive computer application program for organizing,<br>
analyzing, and storing data.<br>

<b>Roster method</b><br>
<b>In a roster or listing method, the elements</b><br>
of a set are listed inside the curly brackets.<br>
<b>The elements are separated by using</b><br>
commas. For example, if B is a set of all<br>
factors of 42, then it can be represented<br>
as B = {1, 2, 3, 6, 7, 14, 21, 42}.<br>
<b>Set builder notation</b><br>
<b>This is a method that uses formulas</b><br>
to describe sets. The elements of the<br>
set are described by using symbols,<br>
usually x followed by a colon (:) which<br>
is read as ŌĆ£such thatŌĆØ, and followed by<br>
a description of the elements. The whole<br>
<b>Methods of representing sets</b><br>
<b>The useful methods which are used</b><br>
to represent sets are descriptive or<br>
statement form method, roster or listing<br>
form method, and rule or set builder<br>
notation.<br>
<b>Descriptive method</b><br>
<b>In this method, the elements of a set are</b><br>
described by words and the description is<br>
enclosed in curly brackets. For instance,<br>
if A is the set of positive even numbers<br>
less than or equal to twenty, it can be<br>
written as A = {positive even numbers<br>
less than or equal to twenty}.<br>
<b>Sets</b><br>
<b>Introduction</b><br>
A set is a collection of objects which have common properties. The set is usually<br>
denoted by a capital letter, and its objects are listed between curly brackets<br>
{ } separated by commas. Each object inside the set is called an element of<br>
that set. Some basic mathematical concepts, such as relations and vectors,<br>
are precisely specified using theoretical concepts of sets. In this chapter, you<br>
will learn about the number of elements in the set, basic set operations, Venn<br>
diagrams, and simplification of set expressions. The competencies developed<br>
can be applied to perform various tasks such as organizing, creating, and<br>
categorizing objects.<br>

<b>Two</b><br>
<b>Chapter</b><br>

<b>Types of sets</b><br>
<b>There are various types of sets. These</b><br>
include the empty or null set, equal sets,<br>
equivalent sets, subsets, finite and infinite<br>
sets, proper and improper subsets,<br>
supersets, power sets, singleton set,<br>
and universal set.<br>
<b>Empty or null set</b><br>

an empty set because there is no<br>

<b>Equal sets</b><br>
<b>Two or more sets are said to be┬Āequal,┬Āif</b><br>
they have the same elements and the<br>
same number of elements and elements<br>
must be alike. For instance,  if  A = {1,<br>
2, 3, 4, 5} and  B = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}, then A =<br>
<b>B. The two sets are equal meaning that</b><br>
the same elements in set A are found in<br>
set B, and the two sets have the same<br>
number of elements. The comparison<br>
written as A=B and it is read as set A is<br>
equal to set B.<br>
<b>Unequal sets are sets which have at least</b><br>
one uncommon element.  Unequal sets<br>
<b>C and D are denoted by C</b><br>
<b>D.</b><br>
ŌēĀ<br>
<b>For example, if C = {a, b, c, d, e, g} and</b><br>
<b>D = {a, b, c, d, e, f}, then sets C and D</b><br>
are not  equal.<br>
<b>Note that, not all elements in set C are</b><br>
in set D and vice versa, although the two<br>
sets have the same number of elements.<br>
<b>Therefore, C</b><br>
<b>D</b><br>
ŌēĀ<br>
.<br>
<b>Example 2.4</b><br>
<b>Given that A = {Halima, Angelina,</b><br>
<b>Christian, Sarapia} and B = {Halima,</b><br>
<b>Angelina, Christian, Sarapia}. Are</b><br>
the two sets equal?<br>
<b>Solution</b><br>
<b>Since each element of set A is in set</b><br>
B and vice-versa, then A = B.<br>
Therefore, the two sets are equal.<br>

<b>D</b><br>
ŌēĀ<br>
.<br>

<b>Equivalent sets</b><br>
<b>Two sets A and B are said to be</b><br>
equivalent if they have the same number<br>
of elements. The elements do not need<br>
to be the same.  What matters is the sets<br>
to have one-to-one correspondence of<br>
elements. Let A = {a, e, i, o, u} and<br>
<b>B = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5},  If the number of</b><br>
elements in sets, A and B are denoted by<br>
n(A) and n(B) , respectively, then sets<br>
A and B are equivalent if n(A) = n(B) .<br>
<b>Set equivalence is denoted by set A</b><br>
<b>B</b><br>
ŌēĪ<br>
.<br>
<b>Example 2.6</b><br>
<b>Given that  S = {x : x is a counting</b><br>
number less than 7} and<br>
G = { x : 1 Ōēż x Ōēż 6, x is an integer}.<br>
<b>Determine whether or not the two</b><br>
sets S and G are equivalent.<br>
<b>Solution</b><br>
<b>Given S = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} and</b><br>
<b>G = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}.</b><br>
<b>Since n(S) = 6 and n(G) = 6, then</b><br>
n(S) = n(G).<br>
<b>Therefore, sets S and G are equivalent</b><br>
sets.<br>

<b>Subsets</b><br>
<b>Set A is said to be a subset of set B if all</b><br>
the elements of set A are also elements<br>
of set B. A symbol for subset is<br>
.<br>
ŌŖé<br>
<b>For instance, if set  F = {all English</b><br>
alphabets} and set G = {all vowels},<br>
then set G is a subset of set F. This is<br>
written as G<br>
<b>F</b><br>
ŌŖé<br>
.<br>
<b>Note that, the number of subsets in a set</b><br>
is given by 2n , where n is the number<br>
of elements in the given set. The terms<br>
ŌĆ£properŌĆØ and ŌĆ£improperŌĆØ subsets are used<br>
in sets. A proper subset, denoted by the<br>
symbol ŌŖé, is a subset containing some of<br>
elements (not all elements) of the original<br>
set, whereas an improper subset denoted<br>
by the symbol ŌŖå is a set containing every<br>
element in the original set. Any set has<br>
one improper subset which is the set itself.<br>
<b>Therefore, given set A, then A ŌŖå A read</b><br>
as ŌĆ£set A is an improper subset of set AŌĆØ.<br>
Other subsets of A are proper subsets.<br>

<b>Power set</b><br>
The collection of all subsets of a set is called the power set. Given set A, the power<br>
set of A is denoted by P(A), and is read as ŌĆ£power set of AŌĆØ. Generally, if n(A)<br>
n<br>
=<br>
then the number of subsets of set A is given by 2n. Thus, P(A) = 2n.<br>

Halima, John}, and {Anna, Ally, Halima, John}.<br>
<b>Finite and infinite sets</b><br>
A finite set is a set with a fixed number of elements which are countable and all<br>
elements can be listed.  An infinite set is a set with uncountable or endless number<br>
elements. In other words, the infinite set is a set that is not finite.<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
59<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
<b>For instance,  if  V  = {a, e, i, o, u} and</b><br>
<b>P = {prime numbers}, set V is called a</b><br>
finite set because it contains countable<br>
elements which are five vowels of the<br>
<b>English alphabets. Set P is an infinite set</b><br>
because it contains uncountable prime<br>
numbers.<br>

<b>Universal sets</b><br>
<b>A universal set is a set containing all</b><br>
elements or members of all related sets<br>
without any repetition of elements.<br>

<b>Basic operations of sets</b><br>
Set operations are performed on two or more sets in order to build a relationship.<br>
There are four main set operations which are union of sets, intersection of sets,<br>
complement of a set, and difference of sets.<br>

The symbol ŌĆ£Ōł¬ŌĆØ is used to denote the union of sets. The union of two sets<br>

<b>Complement of a set</b><br>
<b>The complement of a set A, denoted as</b><br>
c<br>
<b>A or A</b><br>
ŌĆ▓<br>
is the set that contains all the<br>

universal set and set A.  This means that,<br>
if A is a subset of a universal set, then<br>
the elements of the universal set which<br>
are not in A form the complement of A.<br>
<b>For instance, if   U = {a, b, c, d,..., z} and</b><br>
A = {a, b, c, d, e}  then A╩╣ = {f, g, h, ..., z}.<br>

<b>Representation of sets on a number line</b><br>
A number line can be used to represent a set of values in a given interval. The<br>
number of elements within the interval cannot be counted or listed because they<br>
are infinite. An interval on the number line can be open, closed, half open, or half<br>
closed.<br>
An open interval is an interval in which the end points are excluded. For example,<br>
if a<br>
b<br>
&lt;<br>
, then the open interval from a to b, denoted by {<br>
}<br>
(<br>
)<br>
or<br>
,<br>
a<br>
x<br>
b<br>
a b<br>
&lt;<br>
&lt;<br>
is a<br>
number line extending from point a to point b, excluding the end points a and b.<br>
<b>A closed interval from point a to point b, denoted by {</b><br>
}<br>
[<br>
]<br>
or<br>
,<br>
a<br>
x<br>
b<br>
a b<br>
Ōēż<br>
Ōēż<br>
is a<br>
number line extending from point a to point b, including the end points a and b.<br>
An interval can include only one end point. This kind of interval is called<br>
half-open or half-closed.  A half-open interval can be left closed, right open<br>
denoted as {<br>
}<br>

<b>Fundamental laws of set algebra</b><br>
The laws of algebra of sets are the properties of set operations and set relations.<br>
These properties provide insight into the fundamental nature of sets. They are<br>
used in writing set expressions in the most simplified form without changing the<br>
meaning of the original value of the expression.<br>
<b>Proof of the fundamental laws of algebra of sets</b><br>
The laws of algebra of sets can be proved using basic operations. Table 2.2 shows<br>
some laws of algebra of sets, where A, B, and C are non-empty sets.<br>
<b>Table 2.2: Laws of algebra of sets</b><br>

(g)	Identity/Domination law<br>
If U is a universal set and A is a finite set, then the following holds:<br>

<b>Venn diagrams</b><br>
<b>Sets can be geometrically shown by</b><br>
drawings called Venn diagrams. Venn<br>
diagrams were introduced by the<br>
<b>English Mathematician John Venn. Venn</b><br>
diagrams consist of overlapping circles<br>
or ovals inscribed in a rectangle which<br>
represents a universal set. Each circle or<br>
oval represents a set. The points inside<br>
a circle┬Ārepresent elements of the set,<br>
while the points outside the boundary<br>
of the circle represent elements that are<br>
not in the set. Venn diagrams provide a<br>
quick way of showing relationships of the<br>
sets and thus, are  useful in presentations<br>
and reports.<br>
<b>For example,</b><br>
{<br>
}<br>
<b>U</b><br>
a, b, c, d, e<br>
=<br>
can be<br>
presented in a Venn diagram as shown<br>
in Figure 2.1.<br>
d<br>
<b>U</b><br>
<b>A</b><br>
e<br>
a<br>
b<br>
c<br>
<b>Figure 2.1: Venn diagram</b><br>
<b>In Figure 2.1, U is the universal set</b><br>
containing the elements a, b, c, d, and<br>
e. A is the set of alphabetical letters a, b,<br>
and c. It is clearly seen that, A is a subset<br>
of U and A consists of the elements d<br>
and e.<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
75<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
<b>If the sets have any element in common,</b><br>
then the ovals overlap. Such sets are called<br>
joint sets. For example, if<br>
{<br>
}<br>
<b>A</b><br>
a, b, c<br>
=<br>
and<br>
{<br>
}<br>
<b>B</b><br>
a, b, d<br>
=<br>
, then the relation betwen<br>
A and B is represented as in Figure 2.2.<br>
<b>Figure 2.2: Joint sets in a Venn diagram</b><br>
<b>If the sets do not overlap in a Venn diagram,</b><br>
they are called disjoint sets. For example,<br>
if<br>
{<br>
}<br>
<b>A</b><br>
a, b<br>
=<br>
and<br>
{<br>
}<br>
<b>B</b><br>
1, 2<br>
=<br>
the relation<br>
between A and B is shown in Figure 2.3.<br>
<b>Figure 2.3: Disjoint sets in a Venn diagram</b><br>
<b>If all elements of set A are also elements</b><br>
of set B, then set A is a subset of set B.<br>
<b>For example, if A = {7, 8, 11} and</b><br>
<b>B = {6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12}, then the Venn</b><br>
diagram which shows set A is contained<br>
inside set B or A is a proper subset of B<br>
as shown in Figure 2.4.<br>
<b>U</b><br>
<b>B</b><br>

12<br>
9<br>
6<br>
10<br>
<b>A</b><br>
<b>Figure 2.4: Subsets in a Venn diagram</b><br>
<b>Operations of sets using Venn diagrams</b><br>
<b>A Venn diagram is designed in such a</b><br>
way that it depicts the sets and how its<br>
subsets are related to each other. The<br>
number of circles and the way they<br>
intersect should focus on the entire<br>
problem. The regions are then labelled<br>
according to the problem specifications.<br>
<b>Usually, the region corresponding to a</b><br>
given set is shown by a shaded region<br>
with the elements belonging to the set.<br>
<b>Intersection of sets</b><br>
<b>The intersection of two or more sets is</b><br>
shown in a Venn diagram by shading the<br>
overlapping region of the given sets. The<br>
shaded region in Figure 2.5 shows the<br>
intersection of sets A and B.<br>
<b>Figure 2.5: Shaded region showing the</b><br>
intersection of sets A and B<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
76<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
<b>Union of sets</b><br>
<b>The union of sets in a Venn diagram is</b><br>
represented by the combined regions<br>
of all sets under consideration. For<br>
example, in Figure 2.6; the union of<br>
sets, A and B, is the shaded region.<br>
<b>Figure 2.6: Shaded region showing the</b><br>
union of sets A and B<br>
<b>Symmetric difference of sets</b><br>
<b>The symmetric difference of two</b><br>
sets A and B is also known as the<br>
disjunctive union set of elements which<br>
belong either to set A or B but not in<br>
the intersection. In set notation, the<br>
symmetric difference of two sets, A and<br>
<b>B is given by</b><br>
(<br>
)<br>
(<br>
)<br>
<b>A B= A</b><br>
<b>B</b><br>
<b>B</b><br>
<b>A .</b><br>
Ōłå<br>
ŌłÆ<br>
Ōł¬<br>
ŌłÆ<br>
<b>This combination of sets is represented</b><br>
by the region that does not include the<br>
intersection in the Venn diagram. For<br>
example, the shaded region in Figure<br>
<b>2.7 represents the symmetric difference</b><br>
of two sets, A and B.<br>
<b>U</b><br>
<b>B</b><br>
<b>A</b><br>
<b>Figure 2.7: Shaded region showing the</b><br>
symmetric difference of sets A<br>
and B<br>
<b>Relative difference</b><br>
<b>The relative difference of set A with</b><br>
respect to set B, that is A ŌĆō B is the region<br>
in A that does not include B. The relative<br>
difference of set A with respect to set B<br>
is the shaded region in Figure 2.8.<br>

<b>Number of elements/cardinality of sets</b><br>
<b>The cardinality of sets is a measure of</b><br>
sets size, that is the number of elements<br>
in the set. Thus if A is a finite set with<br>
n elements, then the cardinality of set<br>
<b>A is n. Therefore, the cardinality of an</b><br>
empty set is zero. The cardinality of a<br>
combined set from two or more sets<br>
can be determined by using a general<br>
formula.<br>
<b>To derive the general formula, consider</b><br>
two arbitrary finite sets A and B enclosed<br>
in a universal set as represented in Figure<br>
2.10.<br>
<b>Figure 2.10: Two enclosed sets in the</b><br>
universal set<br>

<b>Example 2.32</b><br>
A factory has 45 men and 45 women. A survey showed that 60 workers in the factory<br>
were machine operators, 42 workers were cleaners, 30 of the men were operators,<br>
30 of the men were cleaners, 24 of the men were both operators and cleaners and 12<br>
of the women were neither operators nor cleaners. How many workers in the factory<br>
were both operators and cleaners?<br>
<b>Solution</b><br>
Let M and C represent the sets of men and women who are machine operators and<br>
cleaners, respectively, and x represents the number of women who are both machine<br>
operators and cleaners, then the information is summarized in the following figure:<br>

<b>Example 2.33</b><br>
In a certain city, 85% of the people speak English, 40% speak French, and 20%<br>
speak Spanish. Also, 32% speak English and French, 13% speak English and<br>
Spanish, and 10% speak French and Spanish. Find the percentage of people<br>
who speak all the three languages.<br>
<b>Solution</b><br>
Let E, F, and S represent the sets of people who speak English, French, and<br>
Spanish, respectively.<br>

diagram which summarizes the given information is shown as follows:<br>

<b>Example 2.34</b><br>

<b>Example 2.35</b><br>
<b>In a certain class there are 15 girls who</b><br>
like chocolate, 5 girls who are white,<br>
and 6 girls who are intelligent. Every<br>
white girl likes chocolate, 3 intelligent<br>
girls do not like chocolate. If 2 girls<br>
are both white and intelligent,<br>
(a)	present the above information in<br>
a Venn diagram,<br>
(b)	find the number of girls in the<br>
class,<br>
(c)	find the number of girls who are<br>
white but not intelligent.<br>
<b>Solution</b><br>
(a)	The given information can be<br>
represented in the Venn diagram<br>
as follows:<br>
(b)	Number of girls in the class<br>
= 9 + 3 + 2 + 1 + 3<br>
= 18<br>
<b>Therefore, the number of girls in</b><br>
the class is 18.<br>
(c)	3 girls are white but not inteligent.<br>
<b>Example 2.36</b><br>
<b>In a certain school there are 20</b><br>
students who are girls and 20 who are<br>
boys. 15 students study Mathematics<br>
and 16 study Chemistry. 10 girls<br>
study Mathematics and 11 boys<br>
study Chemistry.  If  8 girls and 7<br>
boys study neither Mathematics<br>
nor chemistry, find the number of<br>
students who study:<br>
(a)	both subjects,<br>
(b)	Mathematics but not Chemistry,<br>
(c)	only one subject,<br>
(d)	at least one subject.<br>
<b>Solution</b><br>
<b>Let C  stands for Chemistry</b><br>
<b>M stands for Mathematics</b><br>
<b>Given;</b><br>
<b>Number of girls = 20</b><br>
<b>Number of boys = 20</b><br>
n(M) = 15<br>
n(C) = 16<br>

8.	 In a certain hospital, 150 patients were diagnosed to be suffering from various<br>
diseases as follows: 93 had Covid-19, 90 had Malaria, 64 had Tuberculosis, 32<br>
had Tuberculosis and Covid-19, 60 had Covid-19 and Malaria, 25 had Malaria<br>
and Tuberculosis. Find the number of patients who had all the three diseases.<br>
9.	 A survey on the type of crops grown in a certain village revealed that out<br>
of 210 families, 106 grow rice, 65 grow maize, 48 grow rice and maize, 22<br>
grow millet and maize, 14 grow rice and millet only. The number of families<br>
who grow rice only is twice the number of those who grow millet only and 7<br>
families grow none of the crops. Determine the number of families growing;<br>
(a)  all three crops<br>
(b)  exactly one crop<br>
(c)  at most one crop<br>
(d)  rice or maize but not millet.<br>

<b>Chapter summary</b><br>
1.	 A set is a collection of objects which have common properties.<br>
2.	 Sets can be presented in various forms. The common forms include descriptive<br>
or statement, roster or listing, and set builder notation.<br>
3.	 There are different types of sets, namely; the empty set or null set, equal sets,<br>
equivalent sets, finite and infinite sets, singleton sets, power sets, proper and<br>
improper subsets, and universal sets.<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
86<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
4.	 The basic operations of sets include union of sets, intersection of set, complement<br>
of sets, and difference of sets.<br>
5.	 The common laws of algebra of sets are; Identity law, idempotent law, double<br>
complement law, commutative law, associative law, De MorganŌĆÖs law, absorption<br>
law, distributive law, and complement law.<br>
6.	 The principal set operations are intersection, union, difference, symmetric<br>
difference, and the complement of sets.<br>

14.	 Out of 880 boys in a school, 224 played cricket, 240 played hockey, and<br>
336 played basketball. Of the total, 64 played basketball and hockey, 80<br>
played cricket and basketball, and 40 played cricket and hockey, 24 boys<br>
played all the three games. Determine the number of boys who;<br>
(a)	did not play any game,<br>
(b)	played only one game,<br>
(c)	played cricket or hockey but not basketball,<br>
(d)	played basketball and hockey but not cricket.<br>
15.	 A class contains 15 boys and 15 girls. If 20 students take Science, 14 students<br>
take Mathematics, of the boys, 10 take Science and, 10 take Mathematics,<br>
8 of the boys take both Science and Mathematics, 4 of the girls take neither<br>
Science nor Mathematics. Find the number of students in the class who take;<br>
(a)	both Science and Mathematics,<br>
(b)	Mathematics but not Science,<br>
(c)	exactly one subject,<br>
(d)	neither of the subjects.<br>
16.	 In a certain high school, 100 students were asked to mention the foreign<br>
languages they learn. Their responses showed that 45 students learn Spanish,<br>
28 learn French, 22 learn Chinese, 12 students learn Spanish and French, 8<br>
learn Spanish and Chinese, 10 learn French and Chinese, and 30 students<br>
do not learn any language. Find the number of students who learn;<br>
(a)	all the three  languages,<br>
(b)	exactly one of the three languages,<br>
(c)	at most two of the three languages.<br>

<b>Mathematics, determine the pass percentages in;</b><br>
(a)	all the three  subjects<br>
(b)	at least two subjects,<br>
(c)	Chemistry or Physics but not Mathematics.<br>

18.<br>
Spare parts manufactured by a certain factory were subjected to three types<br>
of defects A, B, and C.  A sample of 4,000 items were inspected and it was<br>

had defect A and B, 2.6% had defect B and C, 2.0% had defect A and C,<br>
1.2% had all the three defects. Find the percentage of items which had,<br>
(a)	none of the defects<br>
(b)	at least one of the defects<br>
(c)	not more than one defect.<br>

<b>Concept of logic</b><br>
<b>Logic in everyday life refers to a</b><br>
correct reasoning. In mathematics,<br>
it is the study of truth and how the<br>
truth of a statement or proposition<br>
can be obtained from mathematical<br>
deduction. In logic, the interest is not<br>
on the statements themselves, but<br>
how the true and false statements are<br>
related to each other. In most cases, the<br>
propositions are represented by letters<br>
like p, q, r, and s.  These propositions<br>
have to be either true (T) or false (F).<br>
<b>Statements</b><br>
<b>In mathematics, a statement or proposition</b><br>
can be defined as a declarative sentence<br>
which can be either ŌĆ£trueŌĆØ or ŌĆ£falseŌĆØ<br>
but not both. For example, ŌĆ£The sum of<br>
interior angles of a rectangle is180┬░ŌĆØ.<br>
The truth value of this statement is false.<br>
<b>The following sentences are statements:</b><br>
(i)	 6<br>
2<br>
9<br>
+<br>
=<br>
(ii)	3<br>
2<br>
5<br>
+<br>
=<br>
(iii)	 Tanga is the capital city of Tanzania.<br>
(iv)	 Lusaka is the largest city in Tanzania.<br>
<b>Logic</b><br>
<b>Introduction</b><br>
The term ŌĆ£logicŌĆØ originates from the Greek word ŌĆ£LogosŌĆØ meaning idea,<br>
word, thought, argument, account, reason, or principle. Logic can be defined<br>
as a science or an art which deals with the study of truth, principles of correct<br>
reasoning, and making good decisions. In this chapter, you will learn about<br>
statements, logical connectives, laws of algebra of propositions, validity of the<br>
arguments, and electrical networks of statements. The competencies developed<br>
have a number of applications in real life situations such as to distinguish<br>
between valid and invalid arguments, correct reasoning and making proper<br>
decisions in daily life activities, constructions of circuit diagrams in the field of<br>
electronics, making judgements in fields of law, among  many other applications.<br>
<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>Three</b><br>
<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>Three</b><br>

<b>Sentences which involve exclamative</b><br>
words, questioning sentences, and<br>
instructional sentences are not<br>
mathematical sentences. If a sentence<br>
mentions a particular place or a particular<br>
person it becomes a statement. For<br>
instance, ŌĆ£Is Juma a lazy boy?ŌĆØ is not<br>
a statement, but ŌĆ£Juma is a lazy boyŌĆØ<br>
is a statement. In writing mathematical<br>
statements, the letters<br>
,<br>
, , ,<br>
p q r s ŌĆ”.<br>
are usually used to denote propositions<br>
in order to reduce the requirement of<br>
writing long sentences. The truth value<br>
of a true proposition is denoted by T and<br>
the truth value of a false proposition is<br>
denoted by F.<br>
<b>Note that, the truth value of a statement</b><br>
is either true or false.<br>
<b>Types of mathematical statements</b><br>
<b>There are two types of mathematical</b><br>
statements, namely; simple mathematical<br>
statements and compound mathematical<br>
statements. A simple mathematical<br>
statement is formed by one declarative<br>
sentence, that is either true or false.<br>
<b>For instance, the statement ŌĆ£Asha is</b><br>
swimmingŌĆØ is a simple statement,<br>
while a compound statement is formed<br>
by more than one declarative sentence.<br>
<b>The statement ŌĆ£If Elia studies hard, then</b><br>
he will pass the testŌĆØ is a compound<br>
statement, since it is formed by two<br>
simple statements which are ŌĆ£Elia<br>
studies hardŌĆØ and ŌĆ£he will pass the testŌĆØ.<br>
<b>The truth value of the statement in</b><br>
(ii) is true, whereas the truth value of<br>
statements in (i), (iii), and (iv) is false.<br>
Note that, not all sentences are statements.<br>
<b>For example;</b><br>
(i)	  What is your name?<br>
(ii)	 Good night daughter.<br>
(iii)<br>
7<br>
12<br>
x ŌłÆ<br>
=<br>
(iv)	  2<br>
5<br>
x<br>
y<br>
z<br>
+<br>
=<br>
<b>The sentences in (i) and (ii) are not</b><br>
statements because they are not<br>
declarative sentences, and the sentences<br>
in (iii) and (iv) are not statements because<br>
they are neither true nor false.<br>
<b>Activity 3.1: Identifying a</b><br>
statement<br>
<b>Individually or in a group, perform</b><br>
the following tasks:<br>
1.	 Construct any six sentences of<br>
your choice.<br>
2.	 From the sentences constructed in<br>
task 1, identify mathematical and<br>
non-mathematical statements.<br>
3.	 Share the results you obtained in<br>
task 2 with other students.<br>
4.	 Discuss with your fellow<br>
students how to differentiate<br>
between a mathematical and a<br>
non-mathematical statements.<br>
5.	 Discuss the challenges you faced<br>
in task 2.<br>

<b>Truth table</b><br>
<b>A truth table is a table that used to show</b><br>
the validity of a compound statement<br>
depending on the truth values of the<br>
simple statements. The number of<br>
cases that describe the given compound<br>
statement depends on the number n<br>
of propositions.  For instance, if the<br>
compound statement has 1 proposition<br>
(<br>
1)<br>
n =<br>
, then the number of cases is 2.<br>
<b>If it has 2 propositions (</b><br>
2)<br>
n =<br>
, then the<br>
number of cases is<br>
2<br>
2 = 4, and if it has<br>
3 propositions (<br>
3),<br>
n =<br>
then the number<br>
of cases is<br>
3<br>
2<br>
8<br>
=<br>
.<br>
<b>Generally, if there are n propositions,</b><br>
then the number of cases is 2n .<br>

<b>Negation of a statement</b><br>
<b>Negation refers to a way of forming</b><br>
a mathematical statement that has the<br>
opposite truth value. For example,<br>
the statement ŌĆ£It is not pŌĆØ is called<br>
the negation of p, mathematically it is<br>
written as ŌĆ£ ~ p ŌĆØ and reads ŌĆ£negation<br>
of pŌĆØ.  One way of writing a negation<br>
statement is to put the word ŌĆ£notŌĆØ with<br>
the verb. Another way is to precede it<br>
with the phrase ŌĆ£it is not true thatŌĆØ or ŌĆ£it<br>
is false thatŌĆØ. For instance, the negation<br>
of the proposition ŌĆ£Today is FridayŌĆØ is<br>
ŌĆ£Today is not FridayŌĆØ or ŌĆ£It is not true<br>
that Today is FridayŌĆØ or ŌĆ£It is false that<br>
<b>Today is FridayŌĆØ.  Similarly, the negation</b><br>
of the proposition ŌĆ£Issa does not love<br>
RitaŌĆØ is ŌĆ£Issa loves RitaŌĆØ.<br>

<b>Logical connectives</b><br>
<b>Logical connectives are symbols used</b><br>
to connect two or more propositions<br>
of a compound statement. Logical<br>
connectives include conjunction,<br>
disjunction, conditional (implication),<br>
and biconditional (double implications).<br>
<b>Conjunction</b><br>
<b>In mathematics, a conjunction is</b><br>
described as a statement formed by<br>
adding two logical statements p and q<br>
with a connector ŌĆ£andŌĆØ, symbolically<br>
denoted by ŌĆ£ Ōł¦ŌĆØ and reads as ŌĆ£andŌĆØ.<br>
<b>When the values of two propositions</b><br>
p and q are combined together by the<br>
connector, it is expressed symbolically<br>
as p<br>
q<br>
Ōł¦<br>
and is read ŌĆ£p and qŌĆØ.  A<br>
conjunction has truth value true if and<br>

<b>Conditional statements (implications)</b><br>
<b>A relationship between two statements in</b><br>
which the second statement is a logical<br>
consequence of the first statement is<br>
referred to as a conditional or implication<br>
statement. If p and q are propositions,<br>
then the implication is written as<br>
ŌĆ£pŌåÆ qŌĆØ,  and its truth value is false when<br>
p is true and q is false, otherwise it is<br>
true. The proposition ŌĆ£pŌåÆ qŌĆØ reads as p<br>
implies q, if p then q, or sometimes q if<br>
p. The truth table of pŌåÆ q is as shown<br>

(x)<br>
ŌĆ£q whenever pŌĆØ<br>
(xi)<br>
ŌĆ£q follows from pŌĆØ<br>
<b>Example 3.8</b><br>

q<br>

.<br>

q :  You can get a job<br>

q<br>

.<br>
<b>Example 3.9</b><br>

q: Angel has a passport<br>

(a)<br>
~<br>
p<br>
q<br>
ŌåÆ<br>

~<br>
p<br>
q<br>
ŌåÆ<br>
(c) ~ p<br>
q<br>
ŌåÆ<br>

(b)	If Angel does not go abroad, then<br>

(c)	If Angel does not go abroad, then<br>
she has a passport.<br>
<b>Biconditional statements</b><br>
<b>A biconditional statement is a statement</b><br>
which is written in the form ŌĆ£ p if and<br>
only if q ŌĆØ. It is denoted by a double-<br>
headed arrow ŌĆ£Ōåö ŌĆØ. If p and q are<br>
propositions, then the biconditional<br>
proposition is written as ŌĆ£ p<br>
q<br>
Ōåö<br>
ŌĆØ<br>
which is read as ŌĆ£p if and only if qŌĆØ.<br>
<b>A biconditional statement is said to be</b><br>
true if and only if both parts have the<br>
same truth value, otherwise it is false.<br>
<b>The truth table of p</b><br>
q<br>
Ōåö<br>
is shown in<br>
Table 3.5.<br>

<b>The following phrases are used to represent</b><br>

p<br>

(iv)	 ŌĆ£If p, then q and converselyŌĆØ<br>
(v)	 ŌĆ£p double implication qŌĆØ<br>
(vi)	 ŌĆ£p implies q and q implies pŌĆØ<br>

1.	 Write the inverse, converse,  and<br>

<b>Logic symbols dominance</b><br>
<b>In algebra, whenever arithmetic operations</b><br>
+, ŌłÆ, ├Ś, and  ├Ę  are used in the expression,<br>
then the expression is evaluated by applying<br>
the BODMAS rule.  That is, Bracket of,<br>
<b>Division, Multiplication, Addition, and</b><br>
<b>Subtraction. In logical expressions,</b><br>
brackets are also used. However, if<br>
brackets in logical expressions are<br>
many, then the order shown in Table 3.10<br>
should be followed. For instance, when<br>
evaluating p<br>
q<br>
r<br>
Ōł¦<br>
Ōł©, workout ŌĆ£Ōł¦ŌĆØ first,<br>
that is (<br>
)<br>
p<br>
q<br>
r<br>
Ōł¦<br>
Ōł©, and not<br>
(<br>
)<br>
p<br>
q<br>
r<br>
Ōł¦<br>
Ōł©<br>
.<br>

expression<br>

<b>Activity 3.2</b><br>
<b>Individually or in a group, perform</b><br>
the following tasks:<br>
1.	 Using the logical connectives Ōł╝,<br>
Ōł¦, Ōł©, ŌåÆ , and Ōåö , construct five<br>
propositions of your choice.<br>
2.	 From the propositions constructed<br>
in task 1, suggest the order of<br>
evaluation using the order of<br>
dominance as shown in Table 3.10.<br>
3.	 Share the results you obtained<br>
in task 2 with other students for<br>
more inputs.<br>
4.	 With your fellow students discuss<br>
the challenges you met in task 2,<br>
if any.<br>
<b>Logical equivalences</b><br>
<b>Two or more logical expressions are</b><br>
logically equivalent if and only if they<br>
have the same truth table. This means,<br>
if p and q are propositions that are<br>
equivalent, then<br>
q<br>
p Ōåö<br>
will have the<br>
truth value T in all cases. A logical<br>
expression or statement having only truth<br>
values true (T) in the last column of its<br>
truth table is called a tautology.<br>

<b>Laws of algebra of propositions</b><br>
<b>The laws of algebra of propositions are sets</b><br>
of pairs of equivalent logical propositions.<br>
<b>The laws are used to simplify complicated</b><br>
logical statements. Table 3.11 presents the<br>
laws of algebra of propositions with their<br>
names.<br>

<b>Arguments</b><br>
An argument is a series of connected propositions that form a definite statement.<br>
Arguments are normally made up of two parts, the initial statements, called<br>
ŌĆ£premisesŌĆØ, followed by a last statement called ŌĆ£conclusionŌĆØ. For instance, the<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
110<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
following is an example of the argument:<br>
ŌĆ£If I go to the cinema, I will not do<br>
my homework. I did my homework.<br>
Therefore, I did not go to the cinemaŌĆØ.<br>
<b>The first two statements ŌĆ£If I go to the</b><br>
cinema, I will not do my homeworkŌĆØ<br>
and ŌĆ£I did my homeworkŌĆØ are premises,<br>
and the last statement ŌĆ£Therefore, I did<br>
not go to the cinemaŌĆØ is the conclusion.<br>
<b>Remember, an argument is valid if and</b><br>
only if whenever all the premises are<br>
true, the conclusion is also true.<br>
<b>Activity 3.3: Identifying premises</b><br>
and conclusion of the argument<br>
<b>Individually or in a group perform</b><br>
the following tasks:<br>
1.	 Construct at least four mathematical<br>
sentences of your choice.<br>
2.	 Using mathematical sentences<br>
constructed in task 1, identify<br>
statements that form some<br>
arguments and hence state the<br>
premises and conclusion for each<br>
of the argument constructed.<br>
3.	 Formulate the mathematical<br>
argument and state the premises<br>
and conclusion drawn from the<br>
argument.<br>
4.	 Share your results with other<br>
students for more inputs.<br>
<b>Testing the validity of an argument</b><br>
<b>If P1, P2, and P3 are premises and q is</b><br>
the conclusion of an argument, then the<br>
full argument is written as<br>
1<br>
2<br>
3<br>
;<br>
;<br>
.<br>
<b>P P</b><br>
<b>P</b><br>
q<br>
Ōł┤<br>
<b>In testing the validity of the argument,</b><br>
connect the premises P1, P2, and P3 with a<br>
conjunction Ōł¦ and connect the premises<br>
with the conclusion by an implication<br>
sign (<br>
).<br>
ŌåÆ If the compound statement<br>
formed is a tautology, then the argument<br>
is valid, otherwise it is not valid, that is,<br>
1<br>
2<br>
3<br>
[<br>
]<br>
<b>P</b><br>
<b>P</b><br>
<b>P</b><br>
q<br>
Ōł¦<br>
Ōł¦<br>
ŌåÆ<br>
must be a tautology<br>
for the argument in question to be valid.<br>
<b>The argument can be valid for false</b><br>
premises and/or conclusion.<br>
