<b>Exercise 3.6</b><br>
<b>Test whether or not each of the</b><br>
following argument is valid:<br>
1.	 If a girl is cute, then she is not<br>
humble. The girl is not humble.<br>
Therefore, she is cute.<br>
2.	 If it rains or snows, then my<br>
rooftop leaks. My rooftop is<br>
leaking. Therefore, it is raining<br>
and snowing.<br>
3.	 I wash the glasses or I do not drink<br>
juice. I drink juice. Therefore, I<br>
wash the glasses.<br>
4.	 My car tyres will get a puncture<br>
if I do not change the tyres<br>
regularly. My car tyres got a<br>
puncture. Therefore, I did not<br>
change my car tyres regularly.<br>
5.	 You will not be treated with<br>
respect if you are not humble.<br>
You are not treated with respect.<br>
Therefore, you are not humble.<br>
6.	 If you are kind to a cat, then it<br>
will be your friend. You were<br>
not kind to a cat. Hence, it is not<br>
your friend.<br>
7.	 If Nairobi is the capital city of<br>
Tanzania, then it is in Tanzania.<br>
Nairobi is not in Tanzania.<br>
<b>Therefore, Nairobi is not the</b><br>
capital city of Tanzania.<br>
8.	 If I am illiterate, then I canŌĆÖt read<br>
and write. I cannot read but I can<br>
write. Thus, I am not illiterate.<br>
9.	 My finger nails will become dirty<br>
if I plant flowers.  My finger nails<br>
did not become dirty. Therefore,<br>
I did not plant flowers.<br>
10.	 People will not fail to understand<br>
me if I speak to them. They did<br>
not understand me. Therefore, I<br>
did not speak to them.<br>
<b>Electrical networks</b><br>
<b>An electrical network is an arrangement</b><br>
of a battery, lamp, and switches in series<br>
or in parallel connection using wires.<br>
<b>Electrical switches are used in electrical</b><br>
networks to allow or stop the flow of<br>
electric current.<br>
<b>Electrical switches and statements</b><br>
<b>When a switch is ŌĆ£ONŌĆØ, the electric</b><br>
current can flow between terminals<br>
and the lamp will be switched ŌĆ£ONŌĆØ.<br>
<b>Otherwise, the switch is ŌĆ£OFFŌĆØ</b><br>
and current cannot flow. The terms<br>
ŌĆ£CLOSEDŌĆØ and ŌĆ£OPENŌĆØ are also used<br>
to mean ŌĆ£ONŌĆØ and ŌĆ£OFFŌĆØ, respectively.<br>
<b>Series and parallel switches</b><br>
<b>Connections of electrical switches can</b><br>
be done in series or in parallel.<br>
<b>Series switches</b><br>
<b>If p and q are two switches connected</b><br>
in series, then the logical expression to<br>
represent the switches is the conjunction<br>
ŌĆ£ p<br>
q<br>
Ōł¦<br>
ŌĆØ. This means that both p and q<br>
must be closed for electricity to flow in<br>
the electrical network. If both switches<br>
are open or one is open and other is<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
114<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
closed, the electric current will not flow.<br>
<b>Figure 3.1(a) and Figure 3.1 (b) shows the</b><br>
series connection of the open and closed<br>
switches. T1 and T2 are terminals bounding<br>
the switches.<br>
(a)<br>
<b>Open switches</b><br>
(b)<br>
<b>Closed switches</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.1 Open and closed switches in series</b><br>
connection<br>
<b>Parallel switches</b><br>
<b>If two switches, p and q are connected</b><br>
in parallel, the logical expression<br>
that represents the two switches is the<br>
disjunction, that is ŌĆ£ p<br>
q<br>
Ōł©<br>
ŌĆØ. This means<br>
that either p or q or both p and q must be<br>
closed for electricity to flow in the electrical<br>
network, otherwise the electric current will<br>
not flow. Figure 3.2 (a) and Figure 3.2<br>
(b) shows the parallel connection of open<br>
switches and closed switches. T1 and T2 are<br>
terminals bounding the switches.<br>
(a)<br>
<b>Open switches</b><br>
(b)<br>
<b>Closed switches</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.2: Open and closed switches in parallel</b><br>
connection<br>
<b>In some applications, the switches can</b><br>
be connected in opposite positions. This<br>
means that, if one switch is open, the<br>
other must be closed, and vice versa. Two<br>
switches that have opposite positions<br>
are called complementary switches.<br>
<b>If these switches are connected in</b><br>
series, electricity will not flow between<br>
two terminals. If they are in parallel,<br>
electricity will flow between the two<br>
terminals. Figure 3.3(a) and Figure<br>
3.3(b) show the two cases.<br>
(a)<br>
(b)<br>
<b>Figure 3.3: Switches connected in opposite</b><br>
positions<br>
<b>Logic can be used in constructing and</b><br>
simplifying networks. It can also be<br>
used in reducing the switches in the<br>
circuit, and hence perform the same<br>
function as a complex circuit. This may<br>
be done using the idea of equivalence<br>
of propositions. In simplifying the<br>
complex electrical network, two steps<br>
are important to follow:<br>
(1) Construct a compound proposition<br>
of the network, and (2) simplify the<br>
compound proposition using the laws<br>
of algebra of propositions.<br>

<b>Chapter summary</b><br>
1.	 Logic is a science or an art which deals with the study of truth, principles<br>
of correct reasoning, and making good decisions.<br>
2.	 A mathematical statement is a declarative sentence which can be either<br>
true or false, but not both.<br>
3.	 The truth value of a true proposition is denoted by a letter ŌĆ£TŌĆØ and the truth<br>
value of a false proposition is denoted by a letter ŌĆ£FŌĆØ.<br>
4.	 There are two types of statements, namely; simple statements and compound<br>
statements.<br>
5.	 The number of cases that describe a given compound statement depends<br>
on the number of propositions contained in the compound statement.<br>
6.	 Negation of a statement is written by putting the word ŌĆ£NOTŌĆØ with the<br>
verb, or to begin the sentence with the phrase ŌĆ£it is not true thatŌĆØ or ŌĆ£it is<br>
false thatŌĆØ.<br>
7.	 A conjunction is a type of compound statement that is comprised of two<br>
propositions joined by the ŌĆ£ANDŌĆØ.<br>
8.	 A disjunction is a compound statement which comprises of two simple<br>
statements formed by joining the statements with the ŌĆ£ORŌĆØ.<br>
9.	 An implication statement is a type of compound statement that is formed<br>
by joining two simple statements with the logical implication connective.<br>
10.	A┬Ādouble implication┬Āstatement denoted by a double-headed arrow ŌĆ£ŌåöŌĆØ is<br>
a type of compound statement formed by a combination of a conditional<br>
statement and its converse. This type of statement is also known as a<br>
biconditional statement. The truth value of the biconditional statement<br>
p<br>
q<br>
Ōåö<br>
is true when simple statements┬Ā p ┬Āand┬Āq┬Āare both true or both false.<br>
<b>Otherwise,</b><br>
p<br>
q<br>
Ōåö<br>
is false.<br>

14.	 Test the validity of each of the following arguments:<br>
(a)	If I am clever, then I know how to reason. I do not  know how to reason.<br>
Therefore, I am not clever.<br>
(b)	Maize grows well whenever there is rain. If the maize grows well, there<br>
is no famine. But there is famine. Therefore, there is no rain.<br>
(c)	If Amos goes to school, then he must have a good life. But he will not have<br>
good life if he fails examinations. Therefore, Amos must go to school.<br>
(d)	The game will be off if it rains or if there are no players. The game was<br>
on. Therefore, it did not rain.<br>
(e)	If my smart phone crashes, I will loose all my contacts. I have not lost<br>
all my contacts. Therefore, my smart phone has not crashed.<br>

<b>Rectangular Cartesian</b><br>
coordinate system<br>
<b>The rectangular Cartesian coordinate</b><br>
system in a plane specifies every point<br>
uniquely by a pair of two coordinates,<br>
x-coordinate which is also referred<br>
as abscissa and y-coordinate which<br>
is also referred to as ordinate.  The<br>
x-coordinate is the perpendicular<br>
distance of a point from the y-axis<br>
which is parallel to the x-axis and<br>
y-coordinate is the perpendicular<br>
distance of the point from the x-axis,<br>
which is parallel to the y-axis. The<br>
axes meet at a point called the origin.<br>
<b>At the origin, the values of x and</b><br>
y-coordinates are always (0,0) as<br>
shown in Figure 4.1.<br>
<b>Figure 4.1: The xy - coordinate system</b><br>
<b>The coordinate system having the same units</b><br>
on both axes is referred to as rectangular<br>
<b>Cartesian coordinate system. Usually, the</b><br>
<b>Coordinate geometry 1</b><br>
<b>Introduction</b><br>
Coordinate geometry is a branch of geometry in which the positions of the<br>
points of geometric figures in two-dimensional plane are defined with the help<br>
of ordered pairs of numbers called coordinates. In this chapter, you will learn<br>
about the rectangular Cartesian coordinate system, ratio theorem, and circle.<br>
The competencies developed will help you to solve problems in the fields of<br>
trigonometry, calculus, and dimensional geometry. Also, in real life, coordinate<br>
geometry is used in space activities such as location of air transport, map<br>
projection, describing the position of objects, and in many other applications.<br>
<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>Four</b><br>
<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>Four</b><br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
125<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
coordinates x and y at any point P in<br>
the coordinate system are represented<br>
as P(x, y).<br>
<b>Plotting points on the Cartesian</b><br>
coordinate system<br>
<b>A point in a plane is represented by a</b><br>
pair of numbers (x, y) and can be plotted<br>
as follows:<br>
(i)	 Draw a perpendicular line from the<br>
x-axis at the point corresponding to<br>
the x value.<br>
(ii)	Draw a perpendicular line to the<br>
y-axis at the point corresponding to<br>
the y value as shown in Figure 4.2.<br>

<b>Area of a rectangle by coordinates of vertices</b><br>
<b>Given four vertices of a rectangle in the Cartesian</b><br>
coordinate system, the vertices can be used to<br>
evaluate the area of the rectangle.<br>

<b>Equations (4.5) and (4.7) can be used</b><br>
to prove that, the opposite sides of a<br>
parallelogram are parallel.<br>
<b>The third property of a parallelogram</b><br>
is that, the opposite interior angles are<br>
equal as shown in Figure 4.5<br>
<b>Activity 4.1: Recognizing that the</b><br>
opposite sides of a parallelogram are<br>
parallel to each other<br>
<b>Individually or in a group, perform each</b><br>
of the following tasks:<br>
1.	 Sketch a parallelogram EFGH with<br>
vertices of your choice.<br>
2.	 Compute the gradients of the line<br>
segments EF, EH, HG, and FG .<br>
3.	 Give a your  opinion regarding the<br>
gradients of each of the following<br>
pairs:<br>
(a)	 EF and HG<br>
(b)	 EH and FG<br>
4.	 Share your findings with your fellow<br>
students for further discussion and<br>
improvements.<br>
<b>From Activity 4.1 it can be observed that,</b><br>
the opposite sides of a parallelogram are<br>
parallel.<br>

<b>Example 4.4</b><br>
<b>The points H, I, J, and K have coordinates</b><br>
(6,6), (16,0), ( 2, 2), ( 12, 8),<br>
ŌłÆ<br>
ŌłÆ<br>
respectively.<br>

bisect each other.<br>
<b>Activity 4.2 Recognizing that the</b><br>

each other<br>

their intersection point.<br>

the intersection of the diagonals<br>
through 180┬░.<br>

of the vertex to the intersection of<br>

comments<br>

<b>Equations of angle bisectors</b><br>
<b>If L1 and L2 are two intersecting lines, and ╬Ė</b><br>
is one of the angles between the lines, then<br>

<b>Locus</b><br>
<b>When a point moves in such a way that it</b><br>
satisfies a given condition, the path it traces is<br>
called a locus. For example, if a point moves<br>
in the plane in such a way that it is always<br>
equidistant from the origin, then its locus<br>
is a circle. Also, if a set of points in three-<br>
dimensional are equidistant from a given point,<br>
its locus is a sphere. If a point (<br>
)<br>
,<br>
x y  moves<br>
so that it satisfies any given condition, it will<br>
describe a locus or curve.<br>
<b>The relation between the coordinate (</b><br>
)<br>
,<br>
x y  of<br>
any point will only be true if the point lies on<br>
the locus.<br>

<b>Ratio theorem</b><br>
<b>A line segment can be divided internally</b><br>
or externally by a point in any given ratio.<br>
<b>In each division, if the ratio is provided</b><br>
then the coordinates of the point dividing<br>
the line segment can be found.<br>

<b>Applications of the ratio theorem</b><br>
<b>The ratio theorem is used in other</b><br>
areas of mathematics such as showing<br>
similarity of two geometrical objects,<br>
and in vector calculations.<br>
<b>A circle</b><br>
<b>A circle is the locus of a point which</b><br>
moves such that its distance from a<br>
fixed point is constant. The fixed point<br>
is called the centre of the circle and the<br>
constant distance is called the radius of<br>
the circle.The diameter of a circle is any<br>
line through the centre of the circle and<br>
with endpoints at the circumference of<br>
the circle. Objects such as a coin, a wheel<br>
of a bicycle or vehicle, camera lenses,<br>
pizzas, Ferris wheels, rings, steering<br>
wheels, and bottle tops, are all examples<br>
of circular objects. If the middle point<br>
of a circular object is exactly located,<br>
then the distance from the middle point<br>
towards any point of the edge will always<br>
be the same.<br>
<b>Standard equation of a circle</b><br>
<b>Suppose C( , )</b><br>
a b  is the point at the centre<br>
of a circle, r is its radius,  and P(x, y)<br>
is any point on the edge of the circle as<br>
shown in Figure 4.13, then the standard<br>
equation of the circle can be obtained.<br>

<b>Equation of a tangent to a circle</b><br>
<b>A tangent to a circle is a straight line which</b><br>
touches the circle at only one point. The<br>
point where it touches the circle is called the<br>
point of tangency. The line tangent to a circle<br>
is always perpendicular to the radius at the<br>
point of tangency as shown in Figure 4.16.<br>
<b>O</b><br>
y<br>
x<br>

circle, (<br>

<b>Equation of a normal to a circle</b><br>
<b>The normal to a circle is a straight line drawn</b><br>
at 90┬║ to the tangent at the point where the<br>
tangent touches the circle. On the other hand,<br>
the normal is referred as a perpendicular line<br>
to the tangent at the point of contact to the<br>
circle as shown in Figure 4.17.<br>

<b>Point of intersection of circles</b><br>
<b>There are three cases in which two circles</b><br>
can intersect. The first case is when<br>
two circles touch each other externally,<br>
the second case is when two circles of<br>
different sizes touch each other internally<br>
and the third case is when two circles<br>
intersect at two distinct points.<br>
(i)	 External point of intersection of<br>
circles<br>
<b>Two circles touch each other externally</b><br>
if and only if the distance between their<br>
centres is equal to the sum of their radii.<br>
<b>Consider two circles touching each other</b><br>
externally as shown in Figure 4.18.<br>

<b>Orthogonal circles</b><br>

<b>For the circles to be orthogonal, triangle</b><br>

<b>That is, the square of the distance</b><br>

<b>Also, the tangent from</b><br>
2<br>
<b>C to the other</b><br>
circle is equal to the radius, R. That is,<br>
if two circles are orthogonal, the length<br>
of the tangent drawn from the centre of<br>
first circle to the second circle is equal<br>
to the radius of the first circle.<br>

<b>Chapter summary</b><br>
1.	 A parallelogram is a quadrilateral<br>
which obey the properties:<br>
(i)<br>
<b>Opposite sides are parallel to</b><br>
each other<br>
(ii)	 The opposite line segments<br>
have equal length<br>
(iii)	 The opposite interior angles<br>
are equal<br>
(iv)	 The diagonals bisect each other.<br>

<b>Graphs of functions</b><br>
<b>A function can be represented</b><br>
graphically. A function has an<br>
independent variable and a dependent<br>
variable. Normally, x is the independent<br>
variable and y is the dependent<br>
variable. To draw a graph of a function,<br>
a common method is to choose some<br>
values for the independent variable ,x<br>
substitute them into the function to get<br>
a set of ordered pair (x, y). Tabulate<br>
the values, and locate the point on the<br>
xy -plane, then, connect the points<br>
to obtain the graph. Normally, using<br>
more points gives a better graph. In<br>
this section, you will learn how to plot<br>
graphs of polynomial functions up to<br>
4th degree, rational functions, composite<br>
functions, exponential functions, and<br>
logarithmic functions.<br>
<b>Activity 5.1:Identifying a function</b><br>
from the drawn graph<br>
<b>Individually or in a group, perform</b><br>
the following tasks:<br>
1.	 Draw each of the following<br>
figures in the xy-plane:<br>
(a)	A parabola opening upwards<br>
or downwards.<br>
(b)	A parabola opening to the<br>
left or to the right.<br>
(c)	A circle of reasonable radius.<br>
<b>Functions</b><br>
<b>Introduction</b><br>
In mathematics, a function is a relation between a set of inputs and a set of<br>
outputs according to a certain rule. Functions have the property that each<br>
input is related to exactly one output. The set of inputs is called the domain,<br>
whereas the set of outputs is called the range. In this chapter, you will learn<br>
how to plot graphs of functions. The competencies developed will help you in<br>
designing machines, making predictions, studying growth relations, formulating<br>
mathematical models, developing computer programs, and in solving many<br>
other problems.<br>
<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>Five</b><br>
<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>Five</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>

<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
2.	 In each of the figures drawn in task 1, draw a vertical line that intersects the<br>
figure at the middle.<br>
3.	 What have you observed after performing task 2?<br>
4.	 What conclusion can you draw from the figures?<br>

+  are<br>
polynomial functions of degree 1, 2, 3, and 4, respectively.<br>
Polynomial functions are classified depending on their degrees, that is:<br>
(i)<br>
If the degree is 0, then the polynomial is a constant function.<br>
(ii)	 If the degree is 1, then the polynomial is a linear function.<br>
(iii)	 If the degree is 2, then the polynomial is a quadratic function.<br>
(iv)	 If the degree is 3, then the polynomial is a cubic function.<br>
(v)	 If the degree is 4, then the polynomial is a quartic function.<br>
<b>Graphs of linear functions</b><br>
<b>A function defined by</b><br>
( )<br>

+<br>
where m is<br>
the gradient, c is the y-intercept,<br>
and x is the independent<br>
variable, is called a linear<br>
function. The graph of a linear<br>
function is always a straight<br>
line, as shown in Figure 5.1.<br>

<b>Graphs of rational functions</b><br>
<b>A function of the form</b><br>

(<br>

(<br>

( )<br>

q x ŌēĀ<br>
is called a<br>
rational function. This kind of functions<br>
may not be defined at some values. The<br>
value at which the rational function is<br>
not defined is known as a hole. As the<br>
values of x get closer to or far from the<br>
hole, the behaviour of the function is<br>
determined.<br>
<b>Asymptote</b><br>
<b>An asymptote to a curve is defined</b><br>
as a curve towards which the curve<br>
approaches as the distance from the<br>
origin increases. There are three types<br>
of asymptotes that may exist in rational<br>
functions, namely; vertical asymptotes,<br>
horizontal asymptotes, and oblique<br>
asymptotes.<br>
<b>A vertical asymptote is a vertical line</b><br>
obtained by equating the denominator<br>
of a rational function to zero, that is<br>
( )<br>
0<br>
q x =<br>
. A horizontal asymptote is a<br>
horizontal line parallel to the axis of<br>
the independent variable. To find the<br>
horizontal asymptotes of the function<br>
( ),<br>
f x<br>
reduce the term with the highest<br>
degree to a constant. This is done by<br>
dividing each term in the rational<br>
function by the variable with highest<br>
degree, and then solve for y when the<br>
independent variable x approaches ┬▒ Ōł×.<br>
<b>Generally, if</b><br>
( )<br>
( )<br>
( )<br>
p x<br>
f x<br>
q x<br>
=<br>
is a rational<br>
function, where p and q are polynomial<br>
functions with leading coefficients a and<br>
b, respectively, then the following hold:<br>
(a)	If the degree of ( )<br>

as the degree of ( ),<br>

<b>An oblique asymptote is a straight line</b><br>
that is inclined at a certain angle with<br>
the x-axis. The oblique asymptote is a<br>
function of the form y<br>
mx<br>
b<br>
=<br>
+  where<br>
0.<br>
m ŌēĀ<br>
<b>This kind of asymptote exists</b><br>
when the degree of the numerator is<br>
greater than that of the denominator.<br>
<b>Normally,  the oblique asymptote is</b><br>
the quotient obtained by dividing the<br>
numerator by the denominator using the<br>
long division method.<br>

<b>Steps for graphing a rational function</b><br>
<b>When graphing a rational function use</b><br>
the following steps:<br>
1.	 Identify the holes, if any.<br>
2.	 Find and locate the x  and<br>
y-intercepts, if they exist.<br>
3.	 Find the asymptotes (horizontal,<br>
vertical, or oblique) if any.<br>
4.	 Draw the asymptotes on the graph,<br>
using dotted lines.<br>
5.	 Sketch the graph by testing the<br>
behaviour of the graph.<br>
<b>Note that, the graph of a rational function</b><br>
may have many vertical asymptotes, but<br>
it will have at most one horizontal or<br>
oblique asymptote.<br>

<b>Composite functions</b><br>
<b>A composite function is a function</b><br>
which is obtained after substituting one<br>
function into another function. Suppose<br>
( )<br>
f x and ( )<br>
g x are two functions, then,<br>
the composite of<br>
( )<br>
f x and ( )<br>
g x is given<br>
as<br>
[<br>
]<br>
( ) .<br>
f g x<br>
<b>The composite function</b><br>
[<br>
]<br>
( )<br>
f g x    is written in short as (<br>
)( )<br>
f<br>
g<br>
x<br>
’ü»<br>
, and read as ŌĆ£<br>
of  of<br>
f<br>
g<br>
xŌĆØ. The function<br>
( )<br>
g x  is the inner function and the<br>
function<br>
( )<br>
f x is the outer function.<br>
<b>Hence,</b><br>
[<br>
]<br>
( )<br>
f g x  can be read as ŌĆ£the<br>
function g is the inner function of<br>
the outer function f ŌĆØ. The composite<br>
function is sometimes written as f<br>
g<br>
’ü»<br>
.<br>
<b>Activity 5.2: Verifying whether a</b><br>
composite of two linear functions<br>
is also a function<br>
<b>Individually or in a group, perform</b><br>
the following tasks:<br>
1.	 Write down any two linear<br>
functions.<br>
2.	 Substitute one function into<br>
another for the functions obtained<br>
in task 1.<br>
3.	 Verify whether the result obtained<br>
in task 2 is a function or not.<br>
<b>Steps to form a composite function</b><br>
<b>The following steps are used to form a</b><br>
composite function:<br>
1.	 Decide which is the inner and<br>
which is the outer function.<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
188<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
2.	 Substitute the inner function into the variable x of the outer function.<br>
3.	 Simplify the resulting composite function.<br>
<b>Example 5.18</b><br>
Give the functions g(x) = 2x ŌĆō 2 and f (x) = x2 + 5, find each of the following:<br>
(a) (<br>
)( )<br>
g<br>
f<br>
x<br>
’ü»<br>

<b>Chapter summary</b><br>
1.	 A function is a relation between a set of inputs and a set of outputs according<br>
to a certain rule.<br>
2.	 A polynomial function is defined by<br>
2<br>
0<br>
1<br>
2<br>

,<br>

<b>Indices and logarithms</b><br>
<b>Indices and logarithms are inter-</b><br>
related areas of algebra in the sense<br>
that they all deal with powers of<br>
numbers. An index is the exponent for<br>
which a base is raised. For instance,<br>
if y, b, and x are any real numbers,<br>
such that<br>
,<br>
x<br>
y<br>
b<br>
=<br>
then<br>
x<br>
b is called a<br>
power with an exponent x (or index<br>
x) and a base b. The index x is called<br>
the logarithm of y to base b, and it is<br>
written as x = logb y. Fractional powers<br>
of positive numbers, in radical sign<br>
are called surds.<br>
<b>Indices</b><br>
<b>An index is a number which shows</b><br>
how many times a quantity has been<br>
multiplied by itself. This number is<br>
usually raised to another number or<br>
variable and is called the exponent. For<br>
example, given<br>
2<br>
10 and<br>
5x , then 2 and<br>
5 are indices of 10 and x, respectively.<br>
<b>In algebra, the laws of indices are used</b><br>
to simplify the expressions involving<br>
indices.<br>
<b>There are several laws of indices which</b><br>
include multiplication, division, power<br>
of zero, brackets, power of products,<br>
<b>Algebra</b><br>
<b>Introduction</b><br>
Algebra deals with the representation of numbers and quantities in formulae<br>
and equations by symbols and letters. In this chapter, you will learn about<br>
indices and logarithms, series, proofs by mathematical induction, roots of a<br>
polynomial function, remainder and factor theorems, inequalities, matrices,<br>
binomial theorem, and partial fractions.  The competencies developed can be<br>
applied in various real-life situations such as in solving problems related to<br>
landscape designing, computer programming, real estate planning, business<br>
and finance management, geometry, budgeting, making a schedule of activities,<br>
cooking, shopping, and in many other fields.<br>
<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>Six</b><br>
<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>Six</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
208<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
negative power, and fractional power. These laws enable to simplify calculations<br>
or expressions involving powers of the same base.<br>
<b>Activity 6.1: Identifying the laws of indices</b><br>
<b>Individually or in a group, perform the following tasks:</b><br>
1.	 List down laws of indices and prove any two laws.<br>
2.	 Construct two questions for each law in task 1.<br>
3.	 Use the laws to solve the respective questions in task 2.<br>
4.	 Share your work with other students for more inputs.<br>
<b>Law of product of powers with the same base</b><br>
When multiplying two terms of the same base, add their indices.<br>
<b>In general,</b><br>
,<br>
m<br>
n<br>
m n<br>
a<br>
a<br>
a<br>
+<br>

<b>Logarithms</b><br>
<b>The logarithm of a number to a given base</b><br>
is the index of the power to which the base<br>
must be raised to produce the number. It is<br>
simply a mirror image of an index. Thus,<br>
the logarithmic function to base a, where<br>
0<br>
a &gt;<br>
is written as<br>
loga<br>
y<br>
x<br>
=<br>
if and<br>
only if<br>
.<br>
y<br>
x<br>
a<br>
=<br>
<b>Laws of logarithms</b><br>
<b>There are helpful laws for rewriting</b><br>
expressions involving logarithms in<br>
different ways but yet in an equivalent<br>
way. These laws are known as the laws<br>
of logarithms. The laws are applicable<br>
to any base, but the same base must be<br>
used throughout the calculation.<br>
<b>Activity 6.2: Recognizing the laws</b><br>
of logarithms<br>
<b>Individually or in a group, perform</b><br>
the following tasks:<br>
1.	 List down the laws of logarithms<br>
you have learnt.<br>
2.	 Prove the laws you have listed<br>
in task 1.<br>
3.	 What have you observed from<br>
the tasks? Give comments.<br>
4.	 List down the challenges you<br>
faced in tasks 1 and 2.<br>
5.	 Share your work with other<br>
students for more inputs.<br>
<b>The following are four important laws</b><br>
of logarithms and their proofs.<br>
<b>Logarithm of a product</b><br>

of the logarithms of those numbers.<br>

<b>Natural logarithms</b><br>
<b>There are two types of logarithms in</b><br>
extensive use. These are the common<br>
logarithms and the natural logarithms.<br>
<b>Common logarithms are base 10</b><br>
logarithms and they are usually written<br>
without showing the base. For example,<br>
10<br>
log<br>
7  and<br>
10<br>
log<br>
2  can be written as<br>
log7  and log 2 , respectively. Natural<br>
logarithms are logarithms with base<br>
e (where e = 2.71828...). Natural<br>
logarithms are written as ŌĆ£loge ŌĆØ, but<br>
often ŌĆ£logeŌĆØ  is replaced by ŌĆ£lnŌĆØ.  For<br>
example, loge x  can also be written<br>
as ŌĆ£ln xŌĆØ. The natural logarithms also<br>
satisfy all laws of logarithms.<br>

<b>Conversion of logarithm from one base</b><br>
to another<br>
<b>One of the properties of logarithms as a</b><br>
powerful method of computation is its<br>
flexibility to change from one base to another.<br>
<b>The change of base formula is used to write</b><br>
a logarithm of a number with a given base<br>
as the ratio of two logarithms each with the<br>
same base that is different from the base of<br>
the original logarithm.<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
216<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
<b>Let b and c be positive numbers not</b><br>
equal to one. Suppose it is required<br>
to determine logb x, then it will be as<br>
follows:<br>
<b>If p = logb x, it implies that</b><br>
x = b p.....................................................(i)<br>

log<br>

c<br>

p<br>

=<br>

<b>Sequences and series</b><br>
<b>The itemized collection of elements in</b><br>
which repetitions are allowed is known<br>
as a sequence, while a series is the sum of<br>
all  elements.<br>
<b>Sequence</b><br>
<b>A set of numbers or algebraic expressions</b><br>
for which a member can be obtained from<br>
a proceeding member by a definite rule<br>
is called a sequence. The numbers that<br>
make up the sequence are called terms of<br>
the sequence. The terms in a sequence are<br>
named by using their positions.<br>
<b>In general, a sequence is usually written as;</b><br>
1<br>
2<br>
3<br>
,<br>

na is the nth term.<br>
<b>The nth term is also called the general term</b><br>
of the sequence, and it represents the last<br>
term of the sequence. In this case, the<br>

<b>Series</b><br>
<b>When the terms of the sequence are</b><br>
linked together with signs of addition<br>
or subtraction, the resulting expression<br>
is known as series. A series is said to be<br>
finite if it is the sum of a finite number of<br>
terms. Otherwise, it is infinite. For instance,<br>
the series,<br>
2<br>
3<br>
1ŌĆō 2<br>
3<br>
4<br>
x<br>
x<br>
x<br>
ŌłÆ<br>

<b>Example 6.14</b><br>
<b>Find the next two terms of the series,</b><br>
1 1<br>
20<br>
210<br>
2300<br>
+ +<br>
+<br>
+<br>
+’üī<br>
<b>Solution</b><br>
<b>By observing the given series, it can be</b><br>
seen that the next term is ten times the<br>
sum of the two consecutive previous<br>
terms.<br>
<b>The 6th term = 10(210 + 2300) = 25,100</b><br>
<b>Similarly,</b><br>
the 7th term = 10(2300 + 25100) = 274,000<br>
<b>Therefore, the next two terms are</b><br>
25,100 and 274,000.<br>
<b>Sigma notation</b><br>
<b>Consider the sum of the first 6 terms of the</b><br>
series 3 + 6 + 9 + 12 + 15 + 18.  This series<br>
can also be written as<br>
3(1) + 3(2) + 3(3) + 3(4) + 3(5) + 3(6).<br>
All terms of this series are of the form 3n.<br>
<b>Thus, 3n is the general term. The series</b><br>
can be defined as the sum of terms of the<br>
form 3n, where n takes all integral values<br>
in order from 1 to 6 inclusive. The Greek<br>
letter Ōłæ called sigma is used to denote<br>
ŌĆ£the sum of termsŌĆØ. Therefore, the series<br>

Activity 6.4: Deducing a formula for the sum of the first n natural numbers<br>
<b>Individually or in a group, perform the following tasks:</b><br>
1.	 Write the formula for the sum of the first n terms of an arithmetic progression.<br>
2.	 Use the formula in task 1 to deduce the formula for the first n natural<br>
numbers.<br>
3.	 What have you observed from tasks 1 and 2? Give comments.<br>
4.	 Share your results with your fellow students for more inputs.<br>

<b>Proofs by mathematical induction</b><br>
Mathematical induction is a method that is used to establish if a given statement<br>
is true for all natural numbers. The method used in mathematical induction is to<br>
establish that the first statement in the infinity sequence of statements is true, and<br>
prove that if any one statement in the sequence is true, then so is the next one.<br>
This concludes that the statement are true for all natural numbers.<br>
In general, proof by mathematical induction is an indirect method of proof which<br>
is used in case a direct method is either not possible or not convenient.<br>
Activity 6.6: Verifying that the method of induction holds true on passing<br>
information through various people from the first to the last<br>
person<br>
<b>In a group, perform the following tasks:</b><br>
1.	 Form a group of a convenient number of students.<br>
2.	 Members of each group should stand in a straight line.<br>
3.	 Let the first student in each line tell his/her secret to the one standing next.<br>
This process has to continue by each student to tell the same secret to the<br>
next student in the line, and stop at the last student.<br>
4.	 What did you observe in each of the following:<br>
(a)	Does the first student in your group know the secret?<br>
(b)	Does everybody in the line know the secret?<br>
(c)	Do you find the last student knowing the secret (if it was not altered<br>
on the way). In each case give reasons.<br>
5.	 What have you observed during this activity? Give reasons.<br>
6.	 Share your findings with other groups for more inputs.<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
229<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
<b>From Activity 6.6, the following</b><br>
observations can be made:<br>
<b>Let n</b><br>
<b>P  be the statement that ŌĆ£</b><br>
n<br>
<b>S students</b><br>
know the secretŌĆØ, and<br>
n<br>
<b>S be the nth</b><br>
student in the line.<br>
<b>S2 knows the secret because it was</b><br>
told by S1. So, P2 is true.<br>
<b>S3 knows the secret because it was</b><br>
told by S2. So, P3 is true. etc.<br>
<b>Sn knows the secret because it was</b><br>
told by Sn-1. So,<br>
n<br>
P is true as well.<br>
<b>Remark: If the secret is unaltered</b><br>
(remained the same), then the secret is<br>
induced from the first student to the last.<br>
<b>At the end, all students in the line know</b><br>
the secret.<br>
<b>The method used is to establish that</b><br>
each person got the secret correctly and<br>
conclude that the secret was induced<br>
from one person to the last, hence proved<br>
by induction.<br>
<b>The proof by mathematical induction is</b><br>
done in three steps as follows:<br>
1.	 Prove that the statement is true for an<br>
arbitrary natural number, say n = 1.<br>
2.	 Assume the statement is true for<br>
n = k.<br>
3.	 Using the assumption made in step<br>
2, prove that the statement is true<br>
for n = k+1.<br>
<b>Generally, the principle of mathematical</b><br>
induction is stated as follows:<br>
<b>If n is a natural number and the statement</b><br>
kP is true for k = 1 and also true for k =<br>
n + 1 then the statement<br>
kP  is true for<br>
all natural numbers.<br>

<b>Roots of a polynomial function</b><br>
Given a polynomial function,  p(x) the values of the independent variable x for<br>
which the polynomial is zero can be determined. This section discusses the general<br>
form of a polynomial function and how its roots can be obtained.<br>
<b>Polynomial function</b><br>
A polynomial function p(x) is well defined as an algebraic expression that takes<br>
the form<br>
1<br>
2<br>
1<br>
2<br>
1<br>
0<br>
( )<br>
,<br>
n<br>
n<br>
n<br>
n<br>
n<br>
n<br>
p x<br>
a x<br>
a<br>
x<br>
a<br>
x<br>
a x<br>
a<br>
ŌłÆ<br>
ŌłÆ<br>
ŌłÆ<br>
ŌłÆ<br>
=<br>
+<br>
+<br>
+<br>
+<br>
+<br>
’üī<br>
where<br>
1<br>
2<br>
1<br>
0<br>
,<br>
,<br>
,<br>
,<br>
,<br>
n<br>
n<br>
n<br>
a a<br>
a<br>
a<br>
a<br>
ŌłÆ<br>
ŌłÆ’üŗ<br>
are real numbers and n is a natural number. The degree<br>
of a polynomial is the highest positive integer exponent of the variable with non-zero<br>
coefficient in the polynomial. Polynomials are named basing on their  degrees. Thus,<br>
a polynomial of degree 1 is called linear, a degree 2 polynomial is called a quadratic,<br>
a degree 3 polynomial is called a cubic, a degree 4 polynomial is called a quartic, a<br>
degree 5 polynomial is called a quintic, and so on.<br>
<b>Roots of polynomials</b><br>
The values of x for which the polynomial, p(x) is zero are called roots or zeros<br>
of p(x).  The roots are called zeros because they are the values of x at which the<br>
polynomial equals to zero.  The highest exponent of a variable gives the maximum<br>
number of roots the polynomial can have. If the highest exponent of a polynomial<br>
is 2, the polynomial can have at most two roots. If the highest exponent is 3, then<br>
the polynomial can have at most three roots, and so on. It is also important to note<br>
that f (x) = 0 is also a polynomial of undefined degree.<br>

<b>Example 6.25</b><br>
Given that p(x) = (x ŌĆō 3) (x2 ŌĆō 1), find the roots of p(x).<br>
<b>Solution</b><br>
The roots of p(x) are obtained when p(x) = 0.<br>
ŌćÆ(x ŌĆō 3) (x2 ŌĆō 1) = 0<br>
ŌćÆ (x ŌĆō 3) (x + 1) (x ŌĆō 1) = 0<br>

Therefore, the roots of p(x) are ŌĆō1, 1, or 3.<br>

coefficients of the equation. Therefore, there are relationships between coefficients<br>
of a quadratic equation and its roots.<br>

<b>Remainder theorem</b><br>
Sometimes it is not easy to find factors of polynomials of degree three or higher<br>
or finding the remainder when polynomials are divided by an expression.<br>
In arithmetic, numbers can be divided using long division method. By this method,<br>
the dividend equals the product of the divisor and quotient plus a remainder.<br>
For example, dividing 627 by 50 gives 12 with a remainder 27. Thus,<br>
627<br>
50 12<br>
27.<br>
=<br>
├Ś<br>
+<br>
This relationship is also applied to polynomials.<br>

<b>Operations on polynomials</b><br>
Mathematical operations such as addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division<br>
can be applied for polynomial functions. The following activity highlights some<br>
operations on polynomials.<br>
Activity 6.7: Recognizing the use of polynomials to model blood flow<br>
Individually or in a group, read the following information, then perform the<br>
tasks that follow.<br>
Cardiac output is the amount of blood pumped through the heart. The output<br>
is measured by a technique called dye dilution. A doctor injects dye into a<br>
vein near the heart and measures the amount of dye in the arteries over time.<br>
The cardiac output of a particular patient can be approximated by the function<br>
3<br>
2<br>

<b>Addition and subtraction of polynomials</b><br>
Addition and subtraction of polynomials is simply done by adding or subtracting<br>
the terms of the same power.┬ĀThe exponent of variable in a polynomial is always<br>
a non-negative integer.<br>

<b>Comparing the coefficients of like terms gives,</b><br>
p = 22, q = 13, r = 8, s = 8, and t = ŌĆō 6.<br>
Therefore, p = 22, q = 13, r = 8, s = 8, and t = ŌĆō 6.<br>

<b>Multiplication of polynomials</b><br>
Multiplication of polynomial is the process of multiplying together two or more<br>
polynomials. This can be performed by applying the distributive property for<br>
multiplication to polynomials. To multiply two polynomials with each other, take<br>
each term of the first polynomial and multiply to the second polynomial.<br>

<b>Division of polynomials</b><br>
Dividing polynomials is an arithmetic operation where a polynomial is divided by<br>
another polynomial with the same or lower degree as compared to the dividend.<br>
Division of polynomials can be done by using two ways namely long division<br>
method and synthetic division method.<br>
<b>Long division of polynomials</b><br>
Division of polynomials can be done using a method similar to long division of<br>
numbers. The following are steps to follow when dividing polynomials by long<br>
division:<br>
1.	 Rewrite the problem in long division form. Make sure that both polynomials<br>
are written in decreasing powers, filling in any missing term with a zero term.<br>
2.	 Divide the first term of the dividend by the first term of the divisor. Place the<br>
quotient to its corresponding like term. Multiply the quotient by the divisor,<br>
place the product below its like terms then subtract.<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
247<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
3.	 Bring down the next term of the dividend.<br>
4.	 Repeat steps 2 and 3 until the last term is obtained.<br>
5.	 Write the final answer. If there is a remainder, place the remainder over the<br>
divisor and add it to the quotient.<br>

<b>Synthetic division method</b><br>
Synthetic division is a simplified method for dividing a polynomial by another<br>
polynomial of degree one. It is performed by writing down only coefficients of<br>
several powers of the variable and changing sign of the constant term in the divisor<br>
so as to replace the usual subtraction by addition.<br>

<b>Inequalities</b><br>
It is common to compare two objects that are not of the same size. For instance,<br>
people say, ŌĆ£She must be older than youŌĆØ or ŌĆ£No way, we cannot be equalŌĆØ or ŌĆ£A<br>
goat is smaller than a cowŌĆØ. These statements compare two objects of different<br>
sizes or unequal sizes. In┬Āmathematics, a statement describing  relationships like;<br>
ŌĆ£greater than, greater than or equal, less than, or less than or equalŌĆØ between two<br>
numbers or algebraic expressions is called inequality.<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
251<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
In general, inequalities are statements that compare two values or sizes. The inequality<br>
symbols include: Ōēż (less than or equal), Ōēź (greater than or equal), &lt; (strictly less<br>
than), and &gt; (strictly greater than).<br>
The inequality that involves variables can be solved and its solution is not a single<br>
value, thus it is a set of values that satisfies the inequality. The method of solving<br>
inequalities is very much like the method used for solving equations. The only<br>
difference is on multiplying or dividing both sides by a negative number. That is,<br>
when both sides of a given inequality are either multiplied or divided by a negative<br>
quantity, the sense of inequality must change (&gt; changes to &lt;,  &lt; changes to &gt;,  Ōēź<br>
changes to Ōēż, and Ōēż  changes to  Ōēź) .<br>

<b>Quadratic inequalities</b><br>
A quadratic inequality is┬Āa polynomial of degree two that uses an inequality sign<br>
instead of an equal sign. There are several ways of solving quadratic inequalities<br>
which  include graphical and algebraic methods. Quadratic inequalities can be<br>
solved graphically by first rewriting the inequality in standard form, with zero on<br>
one side. Sketch the quadratic function and determine where it is above or below<br>
the x-axis. Also, the inequality can be solved algebraically by making use of a sign<br>
chart. To make a sign chart, use the function and test values in each region bounded<br>
by the roots. The concern is to check if the function is positive or negative.<br>

<b>Rational inequalities</b><br>
Rational inequalities are fractions that have a polynomial expression in the<br>
numerator, denominator or both. Generally, a rational inequality compares two<br>
expressions in the form<br>
( )<br>
( )<br>
p x<br>
q x  &lt; 0;<br>
( )<br>
( )<br>
( )<br>
0,<br>
0 or<br>
0<br>
( )<br>
( )<br>
( )<br>
p x<br>
p x<br>
p x<br>
q x<br>
q x<br>
q x<br>
&gt;<br>
Ōēż<br>
Ōēź<br>
,<br>
where ( )<br>
0.<br>
q x ŌēĀ<br>
Note that; the properties of fractions can be applied to simplify rational inequalities,<br>
such as simplifying the expressions by cancelling common factors from the<br>
numerator and the denominator.<br>
<b>The following are steps in solving rational inequalities:</b><br>
1.	 Rewrite the inequality to contain a zero on the right-hand side. Create a single<br>
fraction on the left-hand side.<br>
2.	 Find the critical values of x that make the numerator equal to zero and that<br>
make the denominator equal to zero.<br>
3.	 Construct a table of intervals in which the columns are separated by the critical<br>
values.<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
255<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
4.	 Select a test point in each interval and check to see if it satisfies the original<br>
inequality or the inequality set is less than 0.<br>
5.	 Test the roots of the numerator for their possibility of inclusion to the overall   solution.<br>
Include the roots to get┬Āa true statement if they are part of the overall solution.<br>
6.	 Exclude the roots of the denominator in the overall solution since they make<br>
the rational expression undefined.<br>

<b>Matrices</b><br>
<b>A set of numbers arranged in a rectangular</b><br>
array having m rows and n columns and<br>
enclosed by a square bracket [  ] or round<br>
bracket (    ) is called a matrix. Matrices<br>
are denoted by capital letters A, B, C or<br>
any other letter, and described by using<br>
rows and columns with rows appearing<br>
first. A matrix m n<br>
├Ś  (read as m by n) is<br>
expressed as follows:<br>

<b>Activity 6.9: Recognizing the</b><br>
number of rows and columns in<br>
the matrix<br>
<b>Individually or in a group, read</b><br>
carefully the following paragraph and<br>
then perform the tasks that follow.<br>
<b>An electronic company produces</b><br>
laptops, mobile phones, and desktop<br>
computers. Each laptop requires 10<br>
units of gold, 8 units of copper, and<br>
12 units of iron. Each mobile phone<br>
requires 9 units of gold, 7 units of<br>
copper, and 5 units of iron. Each<br>
desktop computer requires 15, 11,<br>
and 14 units of gold, copper, and<br>
iron, respectively.<br>
1.	 Present this information in matrix<br>
form.<br>
2.	 Identify the number of rows and<br>
columns of the matrix in task 1.<br>
3.	 Identify the order of the matrix<br>
in task 1.<br>
4.	 What are the similarities and<br>
differences between the number<br>
of rows and columns identified<br>
in task 2?<br>
5.	 Share your findings with your<br>
fellow students for more inputs.<br>
<b>Types of matrices</b><br>
<b>There are several types of matrices. The</b><br>
following are some types of matrices:<br>
<b>Row and column matrices</b><br>
<b>A matrix consisting of a single row is</b><br>
called a row matrix or a row vector,<br>
whereas a matrix having a single column<br>
is called a column matrix or a column<br>
vector. For example,<br>

<b>Operations on matrices</b><br>
<b>The operations on matrices include addition,</b><br>
subtraction, and multiplication.<br>
<b>Addition and subtraction of matrices</b><br>
<b>Two or more matrices are added or</b><br>
subtracted if and only if they have the<br>
same order.<br>
<b>Multiplication of a matrix</b><br>
<b>Two matrices can be multiplied if the</b><br>
number of columns in the first matrix<br>
is equal to the number of rows in the<br>
second matrix. The dimension of the<br>
resulting matrix will have the number of<br>
rows of the first matrix and the number<br>
of columns of the second matrix. For<br>
example, if matrix A is of order m<br>
p<br>
├Ś<br>
and matrix B is of order q<br>
n<br>
├Ś<br>
, then<br>
multiplication of the two matrices, AB<br>
is possible if and only if p<br>
q<br>
=<br>
, and the<br>
resulting matrix will be of order<br>
.<br>
m n<br>
├Ś<br>

<b>Determinant of a 3 ├Ś 3 matrix</b><br>
A real number that represents the magnitude of square matrix is called a determinant<br>
of a matrix. The determinant of a matrix A is denoted by det(A) or A . It can be<br>
calculated provided that A is a square matrix. If A<br>
0<br>
=<br>
, then the matrix A is called<br>
singular matrix, and if A<br>
0<br>
ŌēĀ<br>
, then the matrix is called non-singular matrix.<br>
<b>Finding determinants of 3 3</b><br>
├Ś matrices<br>
The following steps can be used in determining the determinant of a 3├Ś3 matrix:<br>
1.	 Choose any row or any column in the given matrix.<br>
2.	 Compute the cofactors of each element in the chosen row or column.<br>
3.	 The determinant is equal to the sum of the products of the elements and<br>
their respective cofactors.<br>

<b>Minors of a matrix</b><br>
The minor of an element in a matrix is the determinant of a sub-matrix formed<br>
by deleting the rows and columns in a given element. Each element in a 3 3<br>
├Ś<br>
matrix has its own minor. The minors may be denoted by Mij, where and<br>
i<br>
j are<br>
the deleted rows and columns, respectively.<br>

<b>Binomial theorem</b><br>
The binomial expression (a + b) can be raised to different powers. When (a + b) is<br>
raised to any power different from 1, the need of having its expanded form arises.<br>
These expansions range from simple expansion when such powers are small to<br>
complex expansion when the powers are large fraction or negative numbers. The<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
286<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
pattern of expansion may give a rule of obtaining the coefficients of terms in an<br>
expansion as it was discovered by a French Mathematician Blaise Pascal.<br>

<b>Binomial</b><br>

<b>Binomial</b><br>

4.   Is it possible to identify the binomial coefficients for (a + b)20? Give reasons.<br>
5.   What did you observe in tasks 1, 2, and 3?<br>
6.   Share your results with other students for more inputs.<br>
The following can be deduced from Activity 6.10.<br>
1.	 The exponent of a is higher on the first term from left and decreases by 1 as<br>
you move to the next terms.<br>
2.	 The exponent of b is zero on the first term from left and increases by 1 as you<br>
move to the next terms.<br>
3.	 The sum of the exponents in each term is n, where n is the value of the power.<br>
4.	 The coefficients are symmetric since they increase from the beginning to the<br>
line of symmetry and then decrease to the end.<br>
5.	 If the coefficients are detached from the expansions, then a triangle is formed.<br>
The resulting triangle is called PascalŌĆÖs triangle.<br>

<b>Figure 6.1: PascalŌĆÖs triangle</b><br>
Two things can be observed from PascalŌĆÖs triangle as seen in Figure 6.1:<br>
(i)	 The rows start with 1 and end with 1<br>
(ii)	 Each coefficient, except the first and the last, is the sum of 2 coefficients to<br>
the left and to the right of the row directly above it.<br>

<b>Binomial expansion</b><br>
The PascalŌĆÖs triangle becomes tedious when the value of the power becomes large.<br>
The easy way to expand the expression of higher power is to apply the binomial<br>
theorem. Actually, binomial theorem is obtained after studying the expansions of<br>
the individual expressions. It is the general expansion that can be used to obtain all<br>
expansions. The binomial theorem states that,<br>
(<br>
)<br>

From Example 6.65, it can be noted that, the denominators of the terms are in a<br>
particular order. The denominators of the 5th, 4th, and 3rd terms are the factorials of 4,<br>
3, and 2, respectively. This is due to the definition of the factorial which states that;<br>
if n is any positive integer, then<br>
( )( )( )<br>

The 1st and 2nd terms have denominators equal to 1. By following the trend from the<br>
3rd term to the 5th term, it is possible to suggest the denominators of the 1st and 2nd<br>
terms in factorial. Since it has been observed that from the 5th term towards the 3rd<br>
term, there is a decrease of the denominator by 1. Hence, the denominators of the 2nd<br>
and 1st terms are 1! and 0!, respectively.<br>

the case when n is negative or fraction, the modified binomial theorem becomes:<br>

The modified binomial theorem is used in the case when n is negative or a fraction.<br>
The series of equation (6.5) does not terminate and it is only convergent with the<br>
limit of its sum as (1 + x)n when x is between ŌĆō1 and 1. This interval is also known<br>
as limits of x for which the expansion is valid.<br>

<b>Partial fractions</b><br>
In arithmetic, it is possible to decompose a fraction like 4<br>
3  into a sum of smaller<br>
fractions like 4<br>
3<br>
=<br>
4<br>
1<br>
2<br>
1 +<br>
. Similarly, a rational expression can be decomposed<br>
into simpler parts known as partial fractions.  For example, (<br>
) (<br>
)<br>
2<br>
1<br>
1<br>
2<br>
+<br>
ŌłÆ<br>
+<br>
x<br>
x<br>
x<br>
can be<br>
decomposed as<br>
2<br>
1<br>
1<br>
1<br>
(<br>
1)(<br>
2)<br>
1<br>
2<br>
x<br>
x<br>
x<br>
x<br>
x<br>
+<br>
=<br>
+<br>
ŌłÆ<br>
+<br>
ŌłÆ<br>
+<br>
.  The fractions<br>
1<br>
1<br>
ŌłÆ<br>
x<br>
and<br>
2<br>
1<br>
+<br>
x<br>
are<br>
called partial fractions of (<br>
) (<br>
)<br>
2<br>
1<br>
1<br>
2<br>
+<br>
ŌłÆ<br>
+<br>
x<br>
x<br>
x<br>
.  The process of taking a rational expression<br>
and decomposing it into simpler rational expressions that can be added or subtracted<br>
to get the original rational expression is called┬Āpartial fraction decomposition.<br>
<b>Decomposition of fraction</b><br>
The process of splitting fractions into its partial fractions is done by the method<br>
of undetermined coefficients. The decomposition techniques differ depending on<br>
the nature of the expression on the denominator.<br>
Decomposition of fractions whose denominators consist of non-repeated<br>
linear factors<br>
If the denominator of a fraction is the product of linear factors or it is an expression<br>
which can be split into linear factors, then the fraction is split into terms of the<br>
form,<br>
1<br>
1<br>
2<br>
2<br>
3<br>
3<br>
<b>A</b><br>
<b>B</b><br>
<b>C</b><br>
a x<br>
b<br>
a x<br>
b<br>
a x<br>
b<br>
+<br>
+<br>
+<br>
+<br>
+<br>

<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
304<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
<b>Observe that, the number of partial</b><br>
fractions to be determined is normally<br>
shown by the factors in the denominator.<br>
<b>The denominators of the partial</b><br>
fractions are the factors. However,<br>
their corresponding numerators are not<br>
known. In this case, the assumption is<br>
that, one constant for each denominator<br>
will be the numerator, then the values of<br>
constants can be computed.<br>
<b>Example 6.78</b><br>
<b>Find the partial fractions of</b><br>
(<br>
) (<br>
).<br>
2<br>
1<br>
6<br>
+<br>
ŌłÆ<br>
x<br>
x<br>
x<br>
<b>Solution</b><br>

<b>Summation of series using partial fractions</b><br>
Some series may be summed using partial fractions. The series<br>
r<br>
u<br>
Ōłæ<br>
is called<br>
a telescopic series if<br>
r<br>
u can be expressed as a sum of terms which appear in the<br>
expression for r k<br>
u + , where<br>
0,<br>
k &gt;<br>
such that some of the terms of the series cancel<br>
on addition, leaving only a few terms which can be easily added. The following<br>
two examples explains on how to use partial fractions in finding the summation<br>
of the series.<br>

<b>Trigonometric ratios</b><br>
Trigonometric ratios are values of trigonometric functions based on the ratio of<br>
sides of a right-angled triangle.<br>
3.	 Use the derived formulae in task<br>
2 to deduce the formulae for<br>
secant, cosecant, and cotangent<br>
of an angle ╬▒.<br>

deduced in tasks 2 and 3.<br>

fellow students for more inputs.<br>
<b>Trigonometry</b><br>
<b>Introduction</b><br>
The word trigonometry comes from the Greek words ŌĆ£trigononŌĆØ which means<br>
triangle and ŌĆ£metronŌĆØ which means to measure. Trigonometry deals with<br>
determination of measures of sides and angles of triangles by means of relevant<br>
trigonometric functions. The trigonometric functions are sine, cosine, tangent,<br>
secant, cosecant, and cotangent of an angle. In this chapter you will learn<br>
about trigonometric ratios, trigonometric identities, compound angle formulae,<br>
double angle formulae, trigonometric equations, factor formulae, radians and<br>
small angles, trigonometric functions, and inverse of trigonometric functions.<br>
The competencies developed can be applied in various real life situations<br>
such as in solving problems related to astronomy, navigation, architecture,<br>
oceanography, and in creation of maps. Generally, trigonometry has great<br>
practical importance to builders, architects, surveyors, engineers, and users<br>
in many other fields.<br>
<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>Seven</b><br>
<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>Seven</b><br>
<b>Activity 7.1: Deducing the formulae</b><br>
of trigonometric ratios<br>
<b>Individually or in a group, perform</b><br>
the following tasks:<br>
1.	 Construct a right-angled triangle<br>
in the xy-plane.<br>
2.	 Deduce the formulae for trigonometric<br>
ratios of sine, cosine and tangent of an<br>
acute angle .<br>

<b>The ratios of sides of a right-angled</b><br>
triangle with respect to any of its acute<br>
angles are known as the trigonometric<br>
ratios of that particular angle. The three<br>
sides of the right-angled triangle are<br>
hypotenuse (the longest side), opposite<br>
(side across from the angle), and adjacent<br>
(base of the angle). Consider the right-<br>
angled triangle OAB in Figure 7.1.<br>

<b>Compound angle formulae</b><br>
<b>The compound angle formula can be</b><br>
used to find the sine, cosine, and tangent<br>
of the sum and difference of angles.<br>
<b>The basic operations on sums and</b><br>
differences of trigonometric functions<br>
can be computed using the concept of<br>
compound angles.<br>

<b>Double angle formulae</b><br>
Double angle formulae are trigonometric identities which express trigonometric<br>
functions of 2╬Ė in terms of trigonometric functions of ╬Ė. They are special cases of<br>
compound angle formulae.<br>
The compound angle formulae under addition of two angles can be used to deduce<br>
the double angle formula.<br>
<b>Considering equation (7.4), sin(A+B)</b><br>
sin AcosB<br>

<b>Half angle formulae</b><br>
Half angle formulae are special trigonometric identities which are derived from<br>
the double angle formulae. These are useful in evaluating trigonometrical ratios<br>
of half angles and deriving other identities which involve half angles.<br>

sin 2A<br>

<b>Factor formulae</b><br>
In algebra, factors are commonly used to solve equations and simplify expressions.<br>
Similarly, in trigonometry, factors are used to factorize the sum and difference of<br>
two terms. Moreover, factors are used to express a product as a sum or difference<br>
of two terms. The factor formulae are applied in solving equations, simplifying<br>
expressions, and in proving trigonometry identities. Factor formulae are deduced<br>
from the compound angle formulae as follows:<br>
<b>From compound angle formulae for sine,</b><br>
sin(A<br>
<b>B)</b><br>
sin Acos<br>

<b>Radians and small angles</b><br>
Radian is a unit of an angle which is defined as the size of the central angle subtended<br>
by an arc of length l equal in length to the radius r of a circle.<br>
<b>Converting degrees to radians</b><br>
<b>Consider Figure 7.6</b><br>

<b>Approximating small angles</b><br>
<b>If the measured angle is small and is</b><br>
measured in radians, the concept of<br>
small-angle approximations can be<br>
used to approximate the values of the<br>
principal trigonometric functions. The<br>
geometry of small angles has some<br>
unique properties.<br>

<b>Domain and range of trigonometric</b><br>
functions<br>
<b>The domain of a given function is the</b><br>
set of all possible inputs (x-values) to<br>
the functions, while the range is the set<br>
of all possible outputs (y-values) of the<br>
function.<br>

<b>Inverse trigonometric functions</b><br>

of any trigonometric ratios. The inverse of sine, cosine, and tangent are defined<br>

<b>Graphs of inverse trigonometric functions</b><br>
The graphs of inverse trigonometric functions differ from those of trigonometric<br>
functions, the roles of<br>
and<br>
y<br>
╬Ė are interchanged. For example, the graph of<br>
1<br>
sin<br>
y<br>
╬Ė<br>
ŌłÆ<br>
=<br>
is a sine curve drawn on the y-axis instead of the x-axis. The domains<br>
of the inverse of trigonometric functions are restricted.<br>

<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
<b>Formulation of linear programming</b><br>
problems<br>
<b>Formulation of a linear programming</b><br>
problem involves the interpretation of<br>
the verbal description of the problem<br>
into algebraic equations or inequalities<br>
aiming at optimizing the scarce<br>
resources available. Formulation of a<br>
linear programming problem requires the<br>
decision variables, objective function,<br>
and constraints.<br>
<b>Decision variables</b><br>
<b>The decision variables are unknown</b><br>
quantities that decide the output. They<br>
are set of quantities that need to be<br>
determined in order to solve the problem.<br>
They represent the ultimate solution.<br>
<b>To solve any linear programming</b><br>
problem, the decision variables need<br>
to be identified. Decision variables are<br>
presented by letters. For instance, let x<br>
represents the number of products of<br>
type A manufactured per month, and<br>
y represents the number of products of<br>
type B manufactured per month.<br>
<b>Objective function</b><br>
<b>An objective function is a linear function</b><br>
whose value is to be either minimized<br>
<b>Linear programming</b><br>
<b>Introduction</b><br>
Most of the production companies and industries focus on optimization of profit<br>
in such a way that operational cost are minimized in order to maximize profit.<br>
Linear programming is a mathematical technique for finding optimal solutions<br>
to problems that can be expressed as linear equations and inequalities. In this<br>
chapter, you will learn about formulation of linear programming problems,<br>
graphical solutions, and transportation problems. The competencies developed<br>
will help you to decide, allocate, select, schedule, and evaluate resources in the<br>
possible way for the purpose of optimizing the available resources especially<br>
in the fields of agriculture, business, engineering, energy, manufacturing, and<br>
transportation.<br>
<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>Eight</b><br>
<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>Eight</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
395<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
or maximized subject to the constraints<br>
defined over the set of feasible solutions.<br>
<b>For instance, in maximization problems,</b><br>
the objective function is written as:<br>
<b>Maximize z = ax + by, while for</b><br>
minimization problems it is  written as:<br>
Minimize z = ax + by.<br>
<b>Constraints</b><br>
<b>Constraints are inequalities or equations</b><br>
which connect the decision variables<br>
under certain restrictions or limitations.<br>
<b>Usually, constraints limit the values of the</b><br>
decision variables due to availability of<br>
resources. For instance, ax<br>
by<br>
c<br>
+<br>
Ōēż for<br>
a maximization problem and ax<br>
by<br>
c<br>
+<br>
Ōēź<br>
for a minimization problem. The constants<br>
a and b are proportional contributions<br>
of each decision variable to both the<br>
objective function and constraints.<br>
<b>Non-negativity constraints</b><br>
<b>The decision variables should always</b><br>
take non-negative values for all linear<br>
programming problems. That is, values<br>
for decision variables should be greater<br>
than or equal to zero.<br>
<b>Example 8.1</b><br>
<b>Two products, P1 and P2 require</b><br>
machines and labours in order to<br>
be produced from a manufacturing<br>
industry. Product P1 requires 1.5<br>
machine hours and 2.5 labour<br>
hours, while product P2 requires<br>
<b>2.5 machine hours and 1.5 labour</b><br>
hours. The available machine hours<br>
is 300 and labour hours is 240 per<br>
month. The profit for P1 is 1,600<br>
<b>Tanzanian shillings and for P2 is</b><br>
1,280 Tanzanian shillings per month.<br>
<b>Formulate a linear programming</b><br>
problem for maximization of profit.<br>

<b>But the profit on the production of the</b><br>
two products has to be maximized.<br>
<b>Thus, the objective function is given</b><br>
by;<br>
<b>Maximize z = 1,600x + 1,280y</b><br>

<b>Exercise 8.1</b><br>
1.	 A paint factory makes two<br>
varieties of paints with standard<br>
quality  P1 and one of high quality<br>
<b>P2. In order to manufacture these</b><br>
paints only two ingredients,<br>
namely; dye and pitch are needed.<br>
<b>P1 requires 2 units of dye and 3</b><br>
units of pitch for each unit made,<br>
and it is sold at a profit of Tshs<br>
2,000 per unit. P2 requires 4 units<br>
of dye and 2 units of pitch for<br>
each unit made, and it is sold at<br>
a profit of Tshs 2,500 per unit.<br>
<b>The factory has stocks of 12</b><br>
units of dye and 10 units of pitch.<br>
<b>Formulate a linear programming</b><br>
problem to maximize the profit.<br>
2.	 A company workshop<br>
manufactures chairs and tables.<br>
<b>Each table requires 4 hours of</b><br>
labour from the construction<br>
department and 2 hours of labour<br>
from the finishing department.<br>
<b>Each chair requires 3 hours of</b><br>
construction and one hour of<br>
finishing. During the current<br>
week, 140 hours of construction<br>
time and 100 hours of finishing<br>
time are available. Each table<br>
produced gives a profit of Tshs<br>
3,250 and each chair gives a profit<br>
of Tshs 3,165. Formulate this<br>
problem as a linear programming<br>
problem to maximize the profit.<br>
3.	 A workshop prints two circuits of<br>
types C1 and C2. Type C1 requires<br>
20 resistors, 10 transistors, and<br>
20 capacitors. Circuit of type<br>
<b>C2 requires 10 resistors, 10</b><br>
transistors, and 30 capacitors.<br>
<b>The workshop has a stock of</b><br>
200 resistors, 120 transistors,<br>
and 150 capacitors. The profit<br>
on each type C1 circuit is Tshs<br>
4,450 and Tshs 3,570 on each<br>
type C2 circuit. Formulate the<br>
linear programming problem for<br>
maximizing profit.<br>
4.	 A small company manufactures<br>
two types of garden chairs. Type<br>
<b>A requires 2 hours of machine</b><br>
time and 5 hours of craftsman<br>
time. Type B requires 3 hours<br>
of machine time and 5 hours of<br>
craftsman time. Each day there<br>
are 30 hours of machine time<br>
and 60 hours of craftsman time.<br>
<b>The profit on each type A chair</b><br>
is Tshs 2,330 and on each type B<br>
chair is Tshs 1,890. Formulate an<br>
appropriate linear programming<br>
problem for maximizing profit.<br>

9.	 A manufacturer has 90, 80, and<br>

running metres of plywood, pine,<br>
and birch, respectively. If product<br>
<b>A is sold at Tshs 10,000 and</b><br>

<b>Formulate a linear programming</b><br>
problem for finding the optimal<br>
growth of the product.<br>

<b>Graphical solution</b><br>
<b>The constraints of the problem can</b><br>
be solved graphically. The solution is<br>
obtained by treating the inequalities as<br>
linear equations but the set of inequalities<br>
will be satisfied by the obtained region. The<br>
following terminologies are useful when<br>
graphing linear programming problems.<br>
<b>Optimal problem</b><br>
<b>Optimal problem is a problem where</b><br>
a particular objective function is<br>
maximized or minimized subject to<br>
constraints over the set of feasible<br>
solutions. It is the problem of finding<br>
the most desirable solution from the<br>
feasible solutions. An optimal problem<br>
may involve maximization of profit of<br>
production, or minimization of cost from<br>
the available resources.<br>
<b>Feasible region</b><br>
<b>A feasible region is the set of all possible</b><br>
feasible solutions. The feasible region<br>
includes also the boundary lines. If the<br>
feasible region is enclosed by a polygon,<br>
it is said to be bounded otherwise, it is<br>
unbounded.<br>
<b>Feasible solution</b><br>
<b>A feasible solution is a solution that</b><br>
satisfies all constraints. Points within<br>
and on the boundary of the feasible<br>
region represent feasible solutions of<br>
the constraints.<br>
<b>Optimal solution</b><br>
<b>An optimal solution is any solution in</b><br>
the feasible region that gives the optimal<br>
value. It is the largest objective function<br>
value for a maximization problem and<br>
smallest objective function value for a<br>
minimization problem.<br>
<b>Optimal value</b><br>
<b>An optimal value is the quantity from</b><br>
the optimal solution that maximizes or<br>
minimizes the objective function of the<br>
linear programming problem.<br>
<b>Optimal point</b><br>
<b>An optimal point is a point where the</b><br>
objective function attains its optimal<br>
value.<br>
<b>Steps for solving linear programming</b><br>
problems graphically<br>
<b>The following are steps of graphing</b><br>
linear programming problems:<br>
1.	 Formulate the linear programming<br>
problem.<br>
2.	 Replace an inequality symbol with<br>
an equal sign to form an equation of<br>
the boundary line of the graph.<br>
3.	 Draw the straight line that is the<br>
boundary line. Use dotted lines if<br>
an inequality sign &lt; or &gt; is included.<br>
<b>Use a solid line if an inequality sign</b><br>
Ōēż or Ōēź is included.<br>
4.	 Identify the feasible region by testing<br>
any convenient point (coordinates)<br>
which does not lie on the boundary.<br>
<b>Substitute the coordinates of the point</b><br>
in the inequality. If the inequality<br>
is satisfied at the tested point, then<br>
the point lies in the feasible region,<br>
otherwise the point does not lie in<br>
the feasible region.<br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

<b>Product B requires 3, 4, and 1 tonnes</b><br>

make 2 units of product A and 7 units of product B.<br>

2.	 A lightweight mountain tents<br>
manufacturing company produces<br>
a standard and an expedition<br>
model. Each standard tent requires<br>
1 hour of cutting and 3 hours of<br>
assembling. Each expedition tent<br>
requires 2 hours of cutting and 4<br>
hours assembling. The maximum<br>
labour hours available per day in<br>
the cutting department and the<br>
assembling departments are 32 and<br>
84, respectively. If the company<br>
makes a profit of Tshs 10,950 on<br>
each standard tent and Tshs 12,750<br>
on each expedition tent, use the<br>
graphical method to determine the<br>
number of tents of each type that<br>
should be manufactured each day<br>
to maximize the daily profit.<br>
3.	 A firm makes two products, P1 and<br>
<b>P2, and has production capacity</b><br>
of 18 tonnes per day. Product P1<br>
requires a production capacity of 2<br>
tonnes per day, while  product  P2<br>

requires a production capacity<br>
of 1 tonne per day. Each tonne<br>
of  P1 and P2 requires 60 hours<br>
of machine work. The maximum<br>
hours available are 720. If the<br>
profit per tonne for P1 is Tshs<br>
14,670 and for P2 is Tshs 13,280,<br>
find optimal solution by graphical<br>
method.<br>
4.	 A farmer can buy two types<br>
of plant food, F1 and F2. Each<br>
cubic metre of F1 contains 30<br>
kg of phosphoric acid, 20 kg of<br>
nitrogen, and 15 kg of potash.<br>
<b>Each cubic metre of F2 contains</b><br>
6 kg of phosphoric acid, 18 kg of<br>
nitrogen, and 24 kg of potash. The<br>
minimum monthly requirements<br>
are 120 kg of phosphoric acid,<br>
180 kg of nitrogen, and 288 kg<br>
of potash. If food F1 costs Tshs<br>
55,000 per cubic metre and food<br>
<b>F2 costs Tshs 70,000 per cubic</b><br>
metre. Use the graphical method<br>
to solve the linear programming<br>
problem to minimize the cost.<br>
5.	 A firm produces two products, P<br>
and Q. The daily total production<br>
limit is 600 units. The firm requires<br>
at least 300 total units that must<br>
be produced every day. Machine<br>
hours consumption per unit is 6<br>
for P and 2 for Q. At least 1,200<br>
machine hours must be used daily.<br>
<b>Manufacturing costs per unit are</b><br>
<b>Tshs 1,500 for P and Tshs 850 for</b><br>
<b>Q. Find an optimal solution for</b><br>
the linear programming problem.<br>
6.	 A carpenter makes two products,<br>
tables and chairs. Processing of<br>
these products is done on machines<br>
<b>A and B.  A chair requires 2 hours</b><br>
on machine A and 4 hours on<br>
machine B. A table requires 4<br>
hours on machine A and 5 hours<br>
on machine B. There are 16 hours<br>
per day available on machine A and<br>
23 hours per day on machine B.<br>
<b>Profits gained by the carpenter from</b><br>
a chair and a table are Tshs 1,570<br>
and Tshs 1,860, respectively. What<br>
should be the daily production of<br>
each of the two products in order<br>
to maximize the profit?<br>
7.	 An agricultural company has 180<br>
tonnes of Nitrogen fertilizers,<br>
160 tonnes of phosphate, and<br>
220 tonnes of potash. It will be<br>
able to sell 3:2:4 mixtures of<br>
these substances at a profit of<br>
<b>Tshs 8,980 per tonne and 2:2:2</b><br>
mixtures at a profit of Tshs 9,880<br>
per tonne respectively. Use the<br>
graphical method to determine<br>
the number of units of these two<br>
mixtures that should be prepared<br>
so as to maximize profit.<br>

8.	 A company manufactures two<br>
products, X and Y. Each product has<br>
to be processed in three departments:<br>
<b>Welding, assembling, and painting</b><br>
departments. Each unit of X takes<br>
2 hours in the welding department,<br>
3 hours in assembling, and 1 hour<br>
in painting. The corresponding<br>
processing hours for a unit of Y<br>
are 3, 2, and 1 hours, respectively.<br>
<b>The labour hours available in a</b><br>
month are 1,500 for the welding<br>
department, 1,500 in assembling and<br>
550 in painting. The contribution<br>
to profit and fixed overheads are<br>
<b>Tshs 19,660 for product X and Tshs</b><br>
21,430 for product Y. Solve this<br>
problem graphically to obtain the<br>
optimal solution for the maximum<br>
contribution for each product.<br>
9.	 A wheat and barley farmer has 168<br>
hectare of ploughed land, and a<br>
capital of Tshs 4,000,000. It costs<br>
<b>Tshs 28,000 to sow one hectare of</b><br>
wheat and Tshs 20,000 to sow one<br>
hectare of barley. Suppose that his<br>
profit is Tshs 160,000 per hectare<br>
of wheat and Tshs 110,000 per<br>
hectare of barley. Use the graphical<br>
method to find the optimal number<br>
of hectares of wheat and barley<br>
that must be ploughed in order to<br>
maximize profit.<br>
<b>Transportation problems</b><br>
<b>In most cases, products or goods</b><br>
produced are transported from the<br>
storage location (source) to the<br>
consumer (destination) at a minimum<br>
cost in order to attain the desired profit.<br>
<b>Since transportation costs are not</b><br>
controllable, then minimizing total cost<br>
requires making the best product routing<br>
decisions in distribution processes. The<br>
transportation problem is a type of<br>
linear programming problem designed<br>
to minimize the cost of distributing a<br>
product from sources to the destinations.<br>
<b>Note that, a transportation problem</b><br>
is considered as a balanced one, that<br>
is, the total supply from the sources to<br>
each destination is equal to the total<br>
demands, otherwise the transportation<br>
problem is unbalanced. Unbalanced<br>
transportation has no feasible region.<br>
<b>There are several kinds of transportation</b><br>
methods which all serve the purpose of<br>
minimization of cost. In this section,<br>
the transportation problems which<br>
involve two sources (S1 and S2) and two<br>
destinations (D1 and D2) are studied.<br>
<b>Also, transportation problems which</b><br>
involve two sources (S1 and S2) and<br>
three destinations (D1, D2 , and D3) are<br>
studied as illustrated in Figure 8.1(a)<br>
and Figure 8.1(b), respectively.<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>

<b>Formulation of transportation</b><br>
problems<br>
<b>Formulation of a transportation problem</b><br>
depends on the number of destinations<br>
from the given sources. Consider the<br>
transportation problem which involves<br>
two sources having k products in source<br>
<b>P and h products in source Q and two</b><br>
destinations which require a products<br>
in destination A and b products in<br>
destination B as shown in Figure 8.2.<br>
<b>Figure 8.2: Two sources and two destinations</b><br>
<b>From Figure 8.2, the decision variables</b><br>
will be defined as follows:<br>
<b>Let: x be the number of products to</b><br>
be transported from source P to<br>
destination A.<br>
y be the number of products to<br>
be transported from source P to<br>
destination B.<br>
<b>Since, there are already products</b><br>
transported from source P to destinations<br>
<b>A and B, the decision variables will</b><br>
deduct the products transported before<br>
as follows:<br>
<b>Let: ( a</b><br>
x<br>

to be transported from source Q to<br>

<b>Similarly, if the transportation problem</b><br>
involves two sources which has k<br>
products in source P and h products in<br>
source Q  and three destinations  which<br>
require a products in destination A,  b<br>
products in destination B, and c products<br>
in destination C as shown in Figure 8.3.<br>

<b>Exercise 8.3</b><br>
1.	 Two timber production centres,<br>
<b>A and B, are capable of</b><br>
manufacturing 120 and 100 pieces<br>
of timber each week, respectively.<br>
<b>Three customers U, V, and W</b><br>
placed weekly orders of 90, 70,<br>
and 60 pieces of timber from<br>
the timber production centres,<br>
respectively. The transportation<br>
costs in Tanzanian shillings are<br>
shown in the following table:<br>
<b>From</b><br>
<b>To</b><br>
<b>A</b><br>

<b>W</b><br>
4,000<br>
5,000<br>
<b>Formulate a linear programming</b><br>
problem for minimization of the<br>
cost of transportation.<br>
2.	 The daily meat requirements of<br>
three hotels L, M, and N is 70 kg,<br>
40 kg, and 60 kg, respectively.<br>
<b>Two meat suppliers, X and Y have</b><br>
the capacities of supplying 90 kg<br>
and 80 kg of meat, respectively.<br>
<b>The costs of transportation in</b><br>
<b>Tanzanian shillings are given in</b><br>
the following table:<br>
<b>From</b><br>
<b>To</b><br>
<b>X</b><br>
<b>Y</b><br>
<b>L</b><br>

2,000<br>

2,000<br>
3,000<br>
<b>N</b><br>
4,000 4,000<br>
<b>Formulate a linear programming</b><br>
problem to minimize the cost of<br>
transportation.<br>
3.	 Two suppliers of sweets, F and<br>
<b>G, are capable of supplying not</b><br>
more than 40 kg and 35 kg of<br>
sweets, respectively. The supplier<br>
is required to supply sweets to<br>
three day care school, X, Y, and<br>
<b>Z. The daily school requirements</b><br>
are 30 kg, 25 kg, and 20 kg for<br>
schools X, Y, and Z, respectively.<br>
<b>The distance in kilometres from</b><br>

hotels are tabulated in the<br>
following table:<br>

<b>B, with 50 tonnes and 45 tonnes</b><br>
