9.	 Mr. Bakari has two warehouses,<br>
<b>W1 and W2 that contain 900</b><br>
tonnes and 600 tonnes of<br>
grained maize, respectively.<br>
<b>He is planning to supply flour</b><br>
to three customers, T1, T2, and<br>
<b>T3, which are in need of  500</b><br>
tonnes, 600 tonnes, and 400<br>
tonnes respectively. Formulate<br>
a linear programming problem<br>
given that the transportation<br>
costs in Tanzanian shillings per<br>
tonne of wheat flour from each<br>
warehouse to each customer are<br>
as shown in the following table:<br>
<b>To</b><br>
<b>From</b><br>
<b>T1</b><br>
<b>T2</b><br>
<b>T3</b><br>
<b>W1</b><br>
600<br>
300<br>
400<br>
<b>W2</b><br>
400<br>
200<br>
600<br>
<b>Solving transportation problems</b><br>
graphically<br>
<b>Transportation problems can be solved</b><br>
graphically similar to the way linear<br>
programming problems are solved.<br>
<b>After a transportation problem is</b><br>
formulated, a graph is drawn to<br>
identify the feasible region and corner<br>
points. The corner points are then used<br>
to obtain the optimal solution of the<br>
given transportation problem.<br>

<b>Example 8.10</b><br>

of goods to Singida and Moshi, respectively. The warehouses at Kigoma and Mtwara<br>
contain 80 and 60 tonnes of goods, respectively. The transportation costs in Tanzanian<br>
shillings per tonne of goods are shown in the following table:<br>
<b>From</b><br>

<b>Exercise 8.4</b><br>
1.	 A cement dealer has two depots<br>
<b>D1 and D2 which hold 120 and</b><br>
40 tonnes, respectively. He has<br>
two customers, C1 and C2 who<br>
have ordered 80 and 50 tonnes<br>
of cement, respectively. Their<br>
distances in kilometres are shown<br>
in the following table:<br>
<b>From</b><br>
<b>To</b><br>
<b>D1</b><br>
<b>D2</b><br>
<b>C1</b><br>
20<br>
40<br>
<b>C2</b><br>
15<br>
30<br>
<b>If the transportation costs are</b><br>
proportional to the distance<br>
travelled per kilometre, how<br>
should the cement dealer<br>
supply the cement to the two<br>
customers to minimize the total<br>
transportation cost?<br>
2.	 A  manufacturer has a warehouse<br>
at Misungwi which contains 25<br>
units of his products. She has<br>
another warehouse at Ngudu<br>
which contains 30 units. She has<br>
to supply to the shops in Mabuki<br>
and Misasi with 20 and 15 units,<br>
respectively. The transportation<br>
costs in Tanzanian shillings per<br>
each unit are tabulated as follows;<br>
<b>From</b><br>
<b>To</b><br>
<b>Misungwi</b><br>
<b>Ngudu</b><br>
<b>Mabuki</b><br>
150<br>
220<br>
<b>Misasi</b><br>
130<br>
180<br>
<b>How should the manufacturer</b><br>
supply her products to minimize<br>
the transportation costs?<br>
3.	 Almasi has made 900 and 600<br>
bricks at his houses H1 and H2,<br>
respectively. He plans to build<br>
new houses at three sites P, Q, and<br>
<b>R. He expects to use 500 bricks</b><br>
at P, 600 bricks at Q, and 400<br>
bricks at R. The transportation<br>
costs in Tanzanian shilling per<br>
brick from each of his house to<br>
each of the three sites are shown<br>
in the following table:<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>

<b>H2</b><br>

4.	 Two factories are located at two<br>

transportation cost?.<br>
5.	 Mrs. Mlunda has two depots,<br>

<b>Chapter summary</b><br>
1.	 Linear programming is a mathematical technique for finding optimal solutions<br>
to problems that can be expressed as linear equations and inequalities.<br>
2.	 The transportation problem is a type of linear programming problem<br>
designed to minimize the cost of distributing products from different sources<br>
to different destinations.<br>
3.	 Decision variables are the unknown quantities that decide the output of the<br>
linear programming problem.<br>
4.	 An objective function is a linear function whose value is to be either<br>
minimized or maximized subject to some constraints defined over the set<br>
of feasible solutions.<br>
5.	 Constraints are inequalities or equations which connect the decision variables<br>
under certain restrictions or limitations on the decision variables.<br>
6.	 A feasible region is a set of all possible solutions of the linear programming<br>
problem.<br>
7.	 An optimal solution is the value at a point where the objective function<br>
reaches its optimal value.<br>
8.	 A feasible solution is solution that satisfies all constraints of the linear<br>
programming problem.<br>
9.	 An optimal value is the quantity from the optimal solution that maximizes<br>
or minimizes the objective function of the linear programming problem.<br>

<b>Derivatives</b><br>
<b>The derivative of a function is defined</b><br>
as the rate of change of a function with<br>
respect to a given variable. Given the<br>
graph of the function, the derivative can<br>
be interpreted as the┬Āslope┬Āof the┬Āgraph┬Āof<br>
the function, or more precisely as the<br>
slope of the┬Ātangent┬Āline┬Āat a point. In<br>
the coordinate plane, slope of a straight<br>
line is given by the┬Āratio┬Āof the change<br>

<b>Differentiation</b><br>
<b>Introduction</b><br>
The concept of differentiation refers to the method of finding the derivative of<br>
a continuous function. It is equivalent to finding the slope or gradient of the<br>
continuous function. For a straight line, the slope remains the same all the way<br>
along the line.  The slope of a curve changes continuously along the curve.<br>
In this case, the gradient of the curve is considered at the point of tangent to<br>
the curve. In this chapter, you will learn about derivatives, differentiation of<br>
functions, applications of differentiation in real life problems, TaylorŌĆÖs and<br>
MaclaurinŌĆÖs series, and introduction to partial derivatives of functions. The<br>
competencies developed are applicable in various real life situations such<br>
as in business, science, engineering, economics, building and constructions,<br>
population modeling, dynamical systems, among many other applications.<br>

<b>Nine</b><br>

<b>The concept of limit as applied in</b><br>
derivatives<br>
<b>The limit of a function f(x) is the value</b><br>
of f(x) as x approaches a certain value.<br>
<b>For example, lim</b><br>
( )<br>
<b>L</b><br>
x<br>
a f x<br>
ŌåÆ<br>
=<br>
means that,<br>
as x approaches a, the value of  f(x) is L.<br>
<b>The notation of limit is used to describe</b><br>
the behaviour of graphs as the variable<br>
approaches a certain point. The slope<br>
of a tangent line to the function can be<br>
defined using the concept of limits. The<br>
following activity illustrates the concept<br>
of limits.<br>
<b>Activity 9.1: Application of limits</b><br>
in derivatives of functions<br>
<b>Individually or in a group, perform</b><br>
the following tasks:<br>
1.	 Sketch the graph of the curve ( )<br>
f x<br>
of your choice on the xy-plane.<br>
2.	 Locate a point Q (x0 ,  f (x0)) of your<br>
choice on the curve.<br>
3.	 Draw a tangent line to the curve<br>
at point Q.<br>
4.	 Pick a point P(x0+ h, f(x0+h), near<br>
(<br>
)<br>
<b>Q</b><br>

( )<br>

5.	 Find the gradient of the secant line<br>
PQ in task 4.<br>
6.	 Discuss how you can use the<br>
gradient of a secant line to<br>
approximate the slope of the<br>
tangent line.<br>
7.	 What did you observe from the tasks?<br>
<b>In Figure 9.2, if h is small, the gradient of</b><br>
the secant line is an approximation of the<br>
gradient of the tangent line. Therefore, it<br>
can be concluded that the gradient of the<br>
tangent line is the limit of the gradient of<br>
the secant line as h becomes smaller and<br>
smaller (means as h approaches zero).<br>
<b>If point P is moved nearer to Q, say</b><br>
to points S, U, V,ŌĆ”, then the chords<br>

closer to the tangent line drawn at point<br>

point Q decreases the value of h. Thus, as<br>
h approaches zero, (that is,<br>
0<br>
h ŌåÆ<br>

<b>Differentiation of a function</b><br>
The process of finding the derivative of a function or rate of change of one variable<br>
with respect to another variable is called differentiation. It is important to note that,<br>
not all functions are differentiable. The function ( )<br>
f x  is said to be differentiable<br>
at a point x if<br>
0<br>
(<br>
)<br>
( )<br>

ŌłÆ<br>
exists. The functions considered in this chapter<br>
are differentiable for all values of x.<br>

<b>Differentiation of implicit functions</b><br>
<b>An implicit function is a function written in</b><br>
terms of both dependent and independent<br>
variables. It is the function of the form<br>
( , ,<br>

<b>Further implicit differentiation of functions</b><br>
implicit differentiation can also be done without rearranging the terms in the<br>

of x by applying the chain rule and the product rule if needed.<br>

<b>Derivatives of trigonometric functions</b><br>
<b>The trigonometric functions involve sine x,</b><br>
cosine x, tangent x, secant x, cosecant x, and<br>
cotangent x and their units are in radians.<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>

<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
Derivatives of trigonometric functions can be derived using first principles of<br>
differentiation.<br>

<b>This section discusses how to determine the derivatives of</b><br>
inverse trigonometric functions.<br>
Activity 9.3: Recognizing derivatives of inverse trigonometric functions<br>
Individually or in a group, perform this activity using the following tasks:<br>
1.	 Think and write down any inverse of trigonometric function.<br>
2.	 Write the formula for differentiation from first principles.<br>
3.	 By showing all steps clearly, differentiate the function in task 1 from first<br>
principles.<br>
4.	 What have you observed from task 3? .<br>
5.	 Think and use any other alternative method of performing task 3.<br>
6.	 Give an opinion between the methods in tasks 3 and 5.<br>

Computer packages in differentiating polynomial and trigonometric functions<br>
Computer packages can be applied to differentiate polynomial and trigonometric<br>
functions. In this section, Maple and MATLAB packages are discussed.<br>
(a)  Maple 18<br>
Maple is a computing software which uses the function ŌĆ£diff ŌĆØ that allows differentiation<br>
of  a function. The ŌĆ£diff ŌĆØ function works similar to that of the function D in Mathematica.<br>
<b>The function ŌĆ£diffŌĆØ in Maple 18 software has two arguments;</b><br>
1.	 The function to be differentiated.<br>
2.	 The independent variable to be considered in differentiation.<br>

(b) MATLAB<br>

change of a quantity with respect to the other, such as, to find the rate at which y<br>
changes with respect to x.<br>

<b>Derivatives of logarithmic functions</b><br>
<b>Logarithmic functions are functions of the form</b><br>
logb<br>
y<br>
x<br>
=<br>
, where<br>
0,<br>
0,<br>
1<br>
x<br>
b<br>
b<br>
&gt;<br>
&gt;<br>
ŌēĀ<br>
It is an inverse of the exponential function. Thus, any exponential function can<br>
be written in logarithmic form. The derivative of logarithmic functions to base e<br>
(natural logarithm) can easily be found. If the base is different from e, it should first<br>
be converted to base e, then the appropriate technique of differentiation is applied.<br>
Activity 9.4:  Recognizing derivatives of logarithmic functions<br>
<b>Individually or in a group, perform the following tasks:</b><br>
1.	 Write down a natural logarithmic function of any independent variable.<br>
2.	 Determine the derivative of the function in task 1 from first principles.<br>
3.	 Determine the derivative of the function in task 1 by using any alternative method.<br>
4.	 Give an opinion between the answers obtained in tasks 2 and 3.<br>
5.	 What have you observed from this activity? Give reasons.<br>

<b>Applications of differentiation</b><br>
<b>Differentiation is useful in solving real</b><br>
life problems particularly in physical<br>
sciences, computer sciences, engineering,<br>
economics, and in many other areas. For<br>
instance, in ecology, derivatives are used<br>
in predictions of the population of species<br>
in a habitat after a certain period of time.<br>
<b>In seismology, it is used to determine</b><br>
earthquakes. In business, the concept of<br>
derivative is used in calculating profit<br>
and loss.<br>
<b>Solving problems involving small changes</b><br>
<b>Small changes of quantities are related</b><br>
to the concept of differentiation. If<br>
x<br>
╬┤<br>
is small, then<br>
y<br>

dy<br>

dx<br>
╬┤<br>
╬┤<br>
Ōēł<br>
. This notation can be written<br>
as<br>
.<br>
dy<br>
y<br>
x<br>
dx<br>
<b>╬ö Ōēł</b><br>
<b>╬ö</b><br>
<b>If y  is a function of x, then the change in</b><br>
y corresponding to a given small change<br>
in x can be determined.<br>
<b>Note that, the symbol ╬┤  can also be</b><br>
written as .╬ö  The notation ╬┤y is read as<br>
ŌĆ£delta yŌĆØ.<br>

<b>Solving problems involving rates of</b><br>
change<br>
<b>The rate of change is defined as the</b><br>
change of one quantity with respect to<br>
another. The derivative of a function<br>
representing the position of a particle<br>
along a line at a time t is the velocity<br>
at that time, and the derivative of the<br>
velocity which is the second derivative<br>
of the position function represents the<br>
acceleration of the particle at time .t<br>
<b>Understanding the nature of change and</b><br>
the rate at which it takes place enable<br>
the expert to make important predictions<br>
and decisions about the atmospheric<br>
pressure, humidity, wind patterns,<br>
temperature, and many others. Also,<br>
it is useful in determination of rates of<br>
change of quantities such as volumes,<br>
areas, motion, and patterns. The chain<br>
rule can be used when dealing with<br>
problems involving rates of changes.<br>
<b>Distance, velocity, and acceleration</b><br>
<b>The concept of rates of change can</b><br>
be used in motion of objects to find<br>
velocity and acceleration when the<br>
displacement function is known.<br>
<b>Let ( )</b><br>

<b>Turning points and points of inflexion</b><br>
of a curve<br>
<b>Turning points are points on a curve</b><br>
where its graph changes direction. The<br>
following activity illustrates the concept<br>
of turning points and point of inflexion.<br>
<b>Activity 9.6: Identification of</b><br>
turning points and points of<br>
inflexion<br>
<b>Individually or in a group, perform</b><br>
the following tasks:<br>
1.	 Draw the graphs of curves of<br>
your choice. The curves should<br>
be of degrees two and three.<br>
2.	 Identify the turning points and<br>
points of inflexion of the graphs<br>
in task 1.<br>
3.	 Identify the values of the turning<br>
points and points of inflexion<br>
found in task 2.<br>
4.	 Use the results in task 3 to<br>
determine the following:<br>
(a)	 Maximum points<br>
(b)	 Minimum points<br>
(c)	 Points of inflexion<br>
5.	 Share your results with other<br>
students for more inputs.<br>
<b>A turning point is a point at which values</b><br>
of the function change from decreasing<br>
to increasing or from increasing to<br>
decreasing. It is a point at which the<br>
gradient of the curve zero. If a curve<br>
is defined by<br>
( )<br>
y<br>
f x<br>
=<br>
, then a turning<br>
point is determined when<br>
( )<br>
0.<br>
f<br>
x<br>
ŌĆ▓<br>
=<br>
<b>In</b><br>
other words, the turning point is obtained<br>
when the derivative of the curve is zero.<br>

<b>Figure 9.3: Turning points and points of inflexion</b><br>
Figure 9.3 illustrates the concepts of turning points and points of inflexion. Points<br>
A and D are maximum points of the curve, point B is a minimum point and points<br>
C and E are points of inflexion. The function has maximum values PA and SD<br>
when<br>
<b>OP</b><br>

x =<br>
and<br>
<b>OT,</b><br>
x =<br>
respectively.<br>
Note that, O is the origin.<br>

<b>Form Five</b><br>
<b>Real life problems involving maximum</b><br>
and minimum values<br>
<b>The turning points and points of inflexion</b><br>
of the functions (curves) have numerous<br>
applications in real life situations. For<br>
instance, stationary points (maximum,<br>
minimum and points of inflexion)<br>
are useful in sketching the curves. In<br>
economics, the stationary points are<br>
used to determine the equilibrium prices,<br>
break-even points, marginal costs and<br>
marginal revenues. The following are<br>
steps for classifying stationary points.<br>
1.	 Given<br>
( ),<br>
y<br>
f x<br>
=<br>
determine dy<br>
dx  (that<br>
is,<br>
( )<br>
y<br>
f<br>
x<br>
ŌĆ▓<br>
ŌĆ▓<br>
=<br>
).<br>
2.	 Let<br>
0<br>
dy<br>
dx =<br>
and solve for the values<br>
of x.<br>
3.	 Substitute the values of x into<br>
the given equation,<br>
( )<br>
y<br>
f x<br>
=<br>
in<br>
order to obtain the corresponding<br>
y-coordinate values. This establishes<br>
the coordinates of the stationary<br>
points.<br>
<b>To determine the nature of stationary</b><br>
points: Either,<br>
4.	 Find<br>
2<br>
2<br>
d y<br>
dx<br>
and substitute into it the<br>
values of x in order to obtain the<br>
nature of the stationary point as<br>
follows:<br>
(a)	 If the value of<br>
2<br>
2<br>
d y<br>
dx<br>
at the point<br>
is positive, then the point is a<br>
minimum.<br>
(b)	 If the value of<br>
2<br>
2<br>
d y<br>
dx<br>
at the<br>
point is negative, then the<br>
point is a maximum<br>
(c)	 If the value of<br>
2<br>
2<br>
d y<br>
dx  at the point<br>
is zero, then the point is a point<br>
of inflexion or maximum or<br>
minimum. To verify this, use<br>
the variation signs of the first<br>
derivatives.<br>
<b>Or</b><br>
5.	 Determine the sign of the gradient of<br>
the curve just before and just after the<br>
stationary points. If the sign of the<br>
gradient of the curve changes from<br>
positive to negative, then the point is<br>
a maximum. If the sign of the gradient<br>
changes from negative to positive,<br>
the point is a minimum. When the<br>
gradient sign does not change, then<br>
the point is a point of inflexion.<br>

<b>Example 9.67</b><br>

<b>TaylorŌĆÖs and MaclaurinŌĆÖs series</b><br>
Taylor's series is an expression of a function f (x) near a point x = a which consists<br>
of an infinite sum of terms involving derivatives of the function at the point x = a,<br>
where a ŌēĀ 0. If we allow a = 0, the infinite series is then known as  Maclaurin's<br>
series.<br>
(a) TaylorŌĆÖs theorem<br>
<b>Consider the polynomial in power of (</b><br>
)<br>

)<br>
.<br>

<b>Introduction to partial derivatives</b><br>
<b>The function</b><br>
( , )<br>
z<br>
f x y<br>
=<br>
depends on two variables x and y , where x  and y are<br>
independent of each other. Thus, f  depends on  x and .y  The derivative of  f<br>
with respect to  x or  y is called a partial derivative.<br>
<b>Identifying functions of two variables</b><br>
The definition of a function of two variables is very similar to the definition for a<br>
function of one variable. The main difference is that, instead of mapping values<br>
of one variable to values of another variable, the ordered pairs of variables are<br>
mapped to another variable.<br>
(a)<br>
( , )<br>
f x y means f is a function of x and y<br>
(b)<br>
( , )<br>
h q r  means h is a function of q and r.<br>

( , )<br>

derivatives of z are obtained as follows:<br>

<b>Integration</b><br>
<b>Inverse process of differentiation</b><br>
<b>Suppose</b><br>
( )<br>
f x  is a known function,<br>
then its derivative f '(x) with respect to<br>
x can be obtained. The inverse process<br>
gives the original function<br>
( )<br>
f x  from<br>
that derivative. This inverse process of<br>
differentiation is called integration.  For<br>

<b>Anti-derivative and integral notation</b><br>
<b>The opposite of the derivative of a</b><br>
function is called the anti-derivative.<br>
<b>The anti-derivative of a function is the</b><br>
integration which is represented by the<br>
integral notation, .Ōł½ When this notation<br>
includes a function ( )<br>
f x  it is written as<br>
( )<br>
f x dx<br>
Ōł½<br>
and read as, ŌĆ£the integral of<br>
( )<br>
f x  with respect to the variable xŌĆØ. In<br>
this case, the function ( )<br>
f x  is called an<br>
integrand, and the change in a variable<br>
is represented by a symbol dx .<br>
<b>Note that, the derivative of a constant</b><br>
is always zero. This implies that,<br>
differentiation makes the function<br>
lose<br>
the<br>
constant.<br>
<b>For</b><br>

<b>Introduction</b><br>
Integration is one of the main operations in calculus and it is an important<br>
concept in Mathematics. Integration is the inverse process of differentiation.<br>
In this chapter, you will learn about inverse process of differentiation,<br>
integration of functions, and applications of integration. The competencies<br>
developed has many real-life applications such as determining the total cost<br>
and total revenue of goods produced, finding displacement, velocity and<br>
acceleration of moving bodies, moment of inertia of vehicles, and the rate<br>
of a chemical reaction. It is also used in building constructions, in graphical<br>
representations where three-dimensional models are demonstrated in analysis<br>
of the spread of infectious diseases, among many other applications.<br>

<b>Ten</b><br>

differentiation the constant becomes 0 as it does not change. Therefore, to show<br>
the presence of a constant, an arbitrary constant is added after the completion of<br>
the integration process. That is,<br>

<b>Types of integrals</b><br>
<b>There are two types of integrals, namely;</b><br>
indefinite and definite integrals.<br>
<b>Indefinite integrals</b><br>

( )<br>

<b>Integration of simple expressions</b><br>
<b>Expressions which involve mathematical</b><br>
operations on simple polynomials, fractions,<br>
and exponential functions are referred to<br>
as simple expressions. Simple expressions<br>
can be integrated by treating each term<br>
separately using the integration rules. The<br>
rules of mathematical operations such<br>
as scalar multiplication, additional, and<br>
subtraction of integral functions are also<br>
used, that is;<br>
(i)<br>
( )<br>

<b>Techniques of integration</b><br>
<b>There are several techniques of  integration</b><br>
depending on the given function. The most<br>
useful techniques of integration include<br>
integration by inspection, by substitution,<br>
by parts, and by partial fractions.<br>
<b>Integration by substitution method</b><br>
<b>This is a method which is used in</b><br>
reversing the chain rule. It is sometimes<br>
called chain rule backward. The following<br>
are steps to be followed when integrating<br>
by substitution method:<br>
<b>Step 1:    Choose a new variable for</b><br>
which the given function is<br>
to be reduced.<br>
<b>Step 2:    Solve for dx of the given</b><br>
integral, where the function is<br>
integrated with respect to x.<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
518<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
<b>Step 3:   Rewrite the integral in terms of</b><br>
the new variable.<br>
<b>Step 4:    Integrate the resulting</b><br>
function with respect to the<br>
new variable.<br>
<b>Step 5:    Substitute back the value of the</b><br>
new variable to get the function<br>
in terms of x.<br>
<b>Consider the following cases where the</b><br>
integration by substitution method is<br>
applicable:<br>
(a)	 Integrals of the form (<br>

Note that, the integrand is in a form of a product of two functions, where the left<br>
part of the integrand is considered as a first function and its right part is considered<br>
as a second function. However, the first function is chosen in such a way that<br>
its derivative can easily be integrated. Generally, the order of preference can be<br>
chosen in such a way that the first function as the function which comes first in<br>
the word ILATE, where I stands for inverse trigonometric functions, L stands for<br>
logarithmic functions, A stands for algebraic functions, T stands for trigonometric<br>
functions, and E stands for exponential functions.<br>
Consider the following cases where the method of integration by parts is applicable.<br>

<b>Reduction formula</b><br>
<b>A reduction formula is regarded as an</b><br>
important method of integration. It helps<br>
to solve complex integration problems<br>
by relying on recurrence relations and<br>
it is used when an expression cannot be<br>
integrated directly. It can also be used<br>
for powers of elementary functions,<br>
trigonometrical functions, logarithmic<br>
functions, and exponential functions, as<br>
well as products of two or more complex<br>
functions. The formula enables to reduce<br>
the degree of the integrand and calculate<br>
the integrals in a finite number of steps.<br>
<b>Consider the problem of finding</b><br>
n<br>
x<br>
nI<br>
x e dx<br>
ŌłÆ<br>
= Ōł½<br>
for<br>
0<br>
n Ōēź<br>
. The process<br>
of integrating by parts reduces the power<br>
of x by 1, each time. This process will<br>
give a formula for<br>
nI . The integral is<br>
integrated as follows.<br>
<b>Let</b><br>
n<br>

<b>Integration using partial fractions</b><br>
<b>When an integrand is a proper rational</b><br>
function, it is resolved into partial<br>
fractions before integrating. The partial<br>
fractions are integrated separately. Table<br>

functions, their partial fractions, and<br>
corresponding integrals.<br>

<b>Integration of trigonometric functions</b><br>
Integration of trigonometric functions depends on the type of the integrand.<br>
(a)	 Integrals of the form sin<br>
cos<br>
ax<br>
bxdx<br>
Ōł½<br>
, sin<br>
sin<br>
,<br>
ax<br>
bxdx<br>
Ōł½<br>
or cos<br>
cos<br>
ax<br>
bxdx<br>
Ōł½<br>
,<br>
where ŌĆ£aŌĆØ and ŌĆ£bŌĆØ are constants.<br>
Integrals which are in this form are evaluated using the factor formula. The factor<br>
formula is used to transform the integrand which is in the form of the product of two<br>
trigonometric functions into a sum of two trigonometric functions. This makes it easier<br>
to integrate the resulting integrands. Table 10.3 shows the factor formulae and their<br>
corresponding integrals given that P and Q are angles containing the variable x.<br>

<b>Example 10.31</b><br>

x dx<br>

.<br>
<b>The integrand is the product of sine</b><br>
and cosine functions. Thus, it can be<br>
expressed as the sum or difference<br>
of two sine functions. In this case,<br>
it will be expressed as a sum of two<br>
sine functions because the sine angle<br>
is greater than the cosine angle.<br>
<b>In this case, the factor formula</b><br>
<b>P</b><br>
<b>Q</b><br>

<b>Q</b><br>
sin P<br>

’ŻĘ<br>
’ŻŁ<br>
’ŻĖ<br>
’ŻŁ<br>
’ŻĖ<br>
can be used.<br>

as follows.<br>
<b>Let P</b><br>

+<br>
=<br>
and P<br>
<b>Q</b><br>

.<br>

and Q<br>

=<br>
.<br>

sin 6<br>

(b)	 Integrals of the form sin<br>

In this form of integrals, the exponent of trigonometric function determines the<br>
approach to be used. Integrals of even exponents of sin x or cos x are integrated<br>
with the help of the double angle formula of cosine which reduce the exponent of<br>
the given trigonometric function. If the integrand contains odd exponent of sin x<br>
or cos x, the exponent is split, and the remaining part is expressed as an even<br>

<b>Note that,</b><br>
1.	 if the power of sine is an odd positive integer then save one sine factor and<br>
convert the remaining factors to cosine. Then, expand and integrate.<br>
2.	 If the power of cosine is an odd positive integer, save one cosine factor and<br>
convert the remaining factors to sine. Then, expand and integrate.<br>
3.	 If the powers of both the sine and cosine terms are even positive integers,<br>
make repeated use of the identities,<br>
2<br>
1<br>
cos2<br>
sin<br>

(c)	  Integrals of the form tan<br>
or<br>
sec<br>
n<br>
n<br>
xdx<br>
xdx<br>
Ōł½<br>
Ōł½<br>
where n is an odd or even integer<br>
In this form of integrals, the following approaches can be used:<br>
1.	 If the power of the tangent is an odd positive integer, save a tangent factor and<br>
convert the remaining factors to secant. Expand and integrate the resulting<br>
function.<br>
2.	 If the power of the secant is an even positive integer, save a secant-squared<br>
factor and convert the remaining factors to tangent. Expand and integrate the<br>
resulting function.<br>
3.	 If there is no secant term and the power of the tangent term is an even positive<br>
integer, convert a tangent-squared factor to secant-squared factor. Expand and<br>
integrate the resulting function.<br>
4.	 If the integral is of the form secn x dx<br>
Ōł½<br>
, where n is odd positive integer, then<br>
use integration by parts.<br>

<b>Integration by splitting the numerator</b><br>
If an integrand is a rational function with a quadratic denominator which cannot<br>
be expressed in simple partial fractions, then it can be expressed in terms of two<br>
fractions by splitting its numerator in the following form:<br>
(<br>

where A and B are constants which split the numerator and<br>
(denominator)<br>
d<br>
dx<br>
is<br>
the derivative of the denominator with respect to the given variable.<br>

<b>Integrals of the form</b><br>

The integral in this form can be easily integrated by splitting the numerator of<br>

(<br>

)<br>

+<br>

<b>Definite integrals</b><br>
<b>When a function is integrated over a</b><br>
specified interval, then the integral is<br>
referred to as a definite integral.<br>

<b>Applications of integration</b><br>
<b>Definite integrals can be used to find the</b><br>
area under a curve and area between two<br>
curves, volume of solid of revolution<br>
about x-axis, about y-axis, and about any<br>
line, length of an arc and to determine<br>
area of a sector.<br>
<b>Area under a curve and area between</b><br>
two curves<br>
<b>Integration can be used to find the</b><br>
area under the curve and area enclosed<br>
between two curves.<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
581<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
<b>Area under a curve and the x-axis</b><br>
When calculating the area under a curve, the first step is to make a sketch of the<br>
curve. Remember that the area lying above the x-axis will have a positive value,<br>
whereas the area lying below the x-axis will have a negative value. In some cases,<br>
the required area at the same time may lie on above and below the x-axis.<br>
Consider Figure 10.5 which shows the curve y = f(x), enclosing the shaded area<br>
with the positive x-axis. If the area is denoted by A, then it is given by<br>
<b>A =</b><br>
( )<br>
( )<br>
b<br>
c<br>
a<br>
b<br>
f<br>
x dx<br>
f x dx<br>
ŌłÆ<br>
Ōł½<br>
Ōł½<br>
where a, b, and c are values of x. These values are usually referred to as limits<br>
when evaluating the area under the curve and between two curves.<br>
Note that, the area under the curve is taken as the magnitude of A. When calculating<br>
the area under a curve y = f (x), follow the following steps:<br>
1.<br>
Sketch the area and identify the area enclosed.<br>
2.<br>
Determine the limits of integration if not given.<br>
3.<br>
Set up the definite integral.<br>
4.<br>
Integrate to get the area.<br>

<b>Area of a region between two curves</b><br>
<b>The area of a region enclosed between</b><br>
the curves is obtained by determining<br>
the integral of the difference between the<br>
two functions using common limits. The<br>
common limits are obtained from the<br>
points of intersection of the two curves.<br>
<b>In Figure 10.7, the area enclosed between</b><br>
the two curves  f(x) and g(x) is given by:<br>

but not Biology.<br>

11.  (a) Halima works hard or she is<br>
poor.<br>
(b)<br>
<b>It is not true that Halima is</b><br>
poor and she works hard.<br>
(c)<br>
<b>If Halima is poor, then she</b><br>
works hard.<br>
(d)<br>
<b>Halima is poor and she works</b><br>
hard.<br>
(e)<br>
<b>If Halima is not poor, then she</b><br>
doesnŌĆÖt work hard.<br>
(f)<br>
<b>Halima is not poor or she</b><br>
doesnŌĆÖt work hard.<br>
(g)<br>
<b>Halima works hard if and only</b><br>
if she is poor.<br>
(h)<br>
<b>Halima does not work hard</b><br>
or she is poor and she works<br>
hard.<br>
12.  (a) Swimming in the poor has danger.<br>
(b)	It is not true that few people have<br>
been drawn in the swimming<br>
pool if and only if  Swimming<br>
in the pool has no danger.<br>
(c)	If swimming in the pool has a<br>
danger, then few people have<br>
been drawn in the Swimming<br>
pool.<br>

(d)<br>
<b>Inverse: If ABC is not an</b><br>
equilateral triangle, then it is not<br>
a right-angled triangle.<br>
<b>Converse: If ABC is a right-</b><br>
angled triangle, then it is an<br>
equilateral triangle.<br>
<b>Contrapositive: If ABC is not a</b><br>
right-angled triangle, then it is<br>
not an equilateral triangle.<br>
(e)<br>
<b>Inverse: If f (x) is not a rational</b><br>
function, then it has no<br>
asymptotes.<br>
<b>Converse: If  f (x) has asymptotes,</b><br>
then it is a rational function.<br>
<b>Contrapositive: If f (x) has no</b><br>
asymptotes, then it is not a<br>
rational function.<br>

<b>Exercise 3.3</b><br>
1.(a)<br>
<b>Inverse:If tomorrow is not</b><br>
<b>Saturday, then Paul will not</b><br>
go to the beach.<br>
<b>Converse: If Paul will go to</b><br>
the beach, then tomorrow is<br>
Saturday.<br>
<b>Contrapositive: If Paul will</b><br>
not go to the beach then<br>
tomorrow is not Saturday.<br>
(b)<br>
<b>Inverse: If it is not raining,</b><br>
then the shop is not closed.<br>
<b>Converse: If the shop is</b><br>
closed, then it is raining.<br>
<b>Contrapositive: If the shop</b><br>
is not closed, then it is not<br>
raining.<br>
(c)<br>
<b>Inverse: Industries are not</b><br>
environmentally friendly, if<br>
they are not in harmony with<br>
the surroundings.<br>
<b>Converse: If the industries</b><br>
are environmentally friendly,<br>
then they are in harmony<br>
with the surroundings.<br>
<b>Contrapositive: If</b><br>
the industries are<br>
not environmentally<br>
friendly, then they are<br>
not in harmony with the<br>
surroundings.<br>

3.  (a) If two vectors are not orthogonal<br>
then their dot product is not zero.<br>
(b)	If the dot product of two vectors<br>
is zero then they are orthogonal.<br>
(c)	If the dot product of two vectors<br>
is not zero then the two vectors<br>
are not orthogonal.<br>

(b)	One cannot be a sailor if one is<br>
not strong.<br>
(c)	If a geometrical figure is not a<br>
rectangle, then it is not a square.<br>

<b>Revision exercise 3</b><br>
1.  Not a mathematical statement.<br>
2.	 (a) Converse: If a person is an adult,<br>
then a person is 20 years old.<br>
<b>Contrapositive: If a person is not an</b><br>
adult, then a person is not 20 years<br>
old.<br>
<b>Inverse: If a person is not 20 years</b><br>
old then a person is not an adult.<br>
(b) Converse: If I have a test today<br>
then, today is Friday.<br>
<b>Contrapositive: If I have no test</b><br>
today then, today is not Friday.<br>
<b>Inverse: If today is not Friday, then</b><br>
I have no test.<br>
(c) Converse: If you are attractive,<br>
then you bought our clothes.<br>
<b>Contrapositive: If you are not</b><br>
attractive then you did not buy our<br>
clothes.<br>
<b>Inverse: If you did not buy our</b><br>
clothes then you are not attractive.<br>

708<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
<b>Glossary</b><br>
<b>Absolute value inequalities</b><br>
an expression with absolute function as well as inequality<br>
signs.<br>
<b>Adjoint of a matrix</b><br>
a transpose of a matrix containing co-factors of elements<br>
of the matrix.<br>
<b>Angle bisector</b><br>
a line that splits an┬Āangle┬Āinto two equal┬Āangles.<br>
<b>Anti-derivative</b><br>
opposite of the derivative of a function.<br>
<b>Argument</b><br>
a series of connected propositions that form a definite<br>
statement.<br>
<b>Asymptote</b><br>
a line that the distance between the curve and the line<br>
approaches zero as one or both of the x or y coordinates<br>
tends to infinity.<br>
<b>Biconditional statement</b><br>
a statement formed by a combination of a conditional<br>
statement.<br>
<b>Binomial theorem</b><br>
an algebraic method of expanding the binomial expression<br>
to any power without lengthy multiplication.<br>
<b>Cartesian coordinate system</b><br>
a system used to determine a point uniquely in xy-plane<br>
through two numbers.<br>
<b>Cofactor of an element</b><br>
the matrix obtained when the column and row of a chosen<br>
element in a matrix are removed.<br>
<b>Column matrix</b><br>
a┬Āmatrix whose elements are arranged in an order of a<br>
single column.<br>
<b>Common logarithms</b><br>
logarithms to base 10, usually written without showing<br>
the base.<br>
<b>Complement of a set</b><br>
a set of elements in the universal that are not in the set<br>
under consideration.<br>
<b>Composite function</b><br>
a function formed by substituting one function into another<br>
function.<br>
<b>Compound angle</b><br>
an algebraic sum of two or more angles which are added<br>
or subtracted through trigonometric functions.<br>
<b>Compound statement</b><br>
a declarative sentence formed by more than one declarative<br>
sentence.<br>
<b>Conditional statement</b><br>
a statement formed by two statements in which the second<br>
statement is a logical consequence of the first statement.<br>
<b>Conjunction statement</b><br>
a compound statement formed by joining two statements<br>
with the connector ŌĆ£andŌĆØ.<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
709<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
<b>Constraints</b><br>
inequalities or equations which connect the decision<br>
variables under certain restrictions or limitations.<br>
<b>Contrapositive of a</b><br>
conditional statement<br>
a statement formed by interchanging the premise and the<br>
conclusion of the inverse statement.<br>
<b>Converse of a conditional</b><br>
statement<br>
a statement formed by exchanging the premises and<br>
conclusion of the original conditional statement.<br>
<b>Definite integral</b><br>
the integral function which has limit of integration.<br>
<b>Derivative of a function</b><br>
the rate of change of a function with respect to a given<br>
variable.<br>
<b>Determinant</b><br>
a scalar value that is a function of the entries of a square<br>
matrix.<br>
<b>Disjunction statement</b><br>
a compound statement formed by joining two statements<br>
with the   connector ŌĆ£orŌĆØ.<br>
<b>Double angle formulae</b><br>
are formulas in trigonometry that deals with the double<br>
angles of trigonometric functions.<br>
<b>Equivalent logical expressions</b><br>
expressions which have the same truth values in the<br>
truth table.<br>
<b>Feasible region</b><br>
the region of the graph which contains all the points which<br>
satisfy all the constraints of the system.<br>
<b>Feasible solution</b><br>
a solution that satisfies all constraints.<br>
<b>Finite set</b><br>
a set with a fixed number of elements.<br>
<b>Hole</b><br>
a removable discontinuity that exist on the graph of<br>
rational function at any input value that causes both the<br>
numerator and denominator of the function to be equal<br>
to zero<br>
<b>Horizontal asymptote</b><br>
a horizontal line parallel to the axis of the independent<br>
variable.<br>
<b>Identity matrix</b><br>
a square matrix in which all elements of the principal<br>
diagonal are 1ŌĆÖs and all other elements are zeros.<br>
<b>Indefinite integral</b><br>
is the integral function which has no limit of integration.<br>
<b>Indices</b><br>
is a number which shows how many times it has been<br>
multiplied by itself.<br>
<b>Infinite set</b><br>
a set containing uncountable number of elements.<br>
<b>Integration by parts</b><br>
a process that finds the integral of the products of functions<br>
in terms of the integral of the product of their derivative<br>
and anti-derivative.<br>
<b>Intersection of sets</b><br>
a set which contains all the elements that are common<br>
to all involved sets.<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
710<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
<b>Inverse of a conditional</b><br>
statement<br>
a conditional statement formed by negating both the<br>
premise and conclusion.<br>
<b>Inverse of a matrix</b><br>
a matrix that, when multiplied by the given matrix gives<br>
the multiplicative identity.<br>
<b>Limits</b><br>
limits describe how a function behaves┬Ānear a point,<br>
instead of at that point.<br>
<b>Locus</b><br>
a set of┬Āpoints┬Āwhose location is determined by one or<br>
more specified conditions.<br>
<b>Logarithms</b><br>
the exponent or┬Āpower┬Āto which a base must be raised to<br>
yield a given number.<br>
<b>Mathematical induction</b><br>
a mathematical technique which is used to prove that if<br>
the statement is true for the nth┬Āiteration, then it is also<br>
true for┬Ā(n+1)th┬Āiteration.<br>
<b>Matrix</b><br>
a set of numbers arranged in a rectangular array, having<br>
m rows and n columns and enclosed by the square or<br>
ordinary brackets.<br>
<b>Minor of determinant</b><br>
the determinant of a sub-matrix formed by deleting the<br>
rows and columns in a given matrix.<br>
<b>Natural logarithms</b><br>
logarithms to the base e (where e =  2.718281828459).<br>
<b>Normal to a circle</b><br>
a┬Āstraight line drawn at 90┬░┬Āto the tangent line at the point<br>
where┬Āthe tangent touches the circle.<br>
<b>Objective function</b><br>
a linear function whose value is to be either minimized<br>
or maximized subject to the constraints defined over the<br>
set of feasible solutions.<br>
<b>Oblique asymptote</b><br>
occurs when the degree of the numerator is greater than<br>
that of the denominator.<br>
<b>Optimal points</b><br>
a point where the objective function attains its maximum<br>
or minimum value.<br>
<b>Optimal value</b><br>
the value from the optimal solution that maximizes<br>
or minimizes the objective function of the linear<br>
programming problems.<br>
<b>Orthogonal circles</b><br>
circles intersecting in such a way that the tangents at the<br>
points of intersection are perpendicular.<br>
<b>Proposition</b><br>
a declarative sentence which can be either true or false<br>
but not both.<br>
<b>Radian</b><br>
the measure of an angle subtended at the centre of a circle<br>
by an arc whose length is equal to the radius of that circle.<br>
<b>Remainder theorem</b><br>
a theorem used to calculate the remainder of the division of<br>
any polynomial by another polynomial without applying<br>
the long division method.<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE  USE ONLY</b><br>
711<br>
<b>Advanced Mathematics for Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Form Five</b><br>
<b>Row matrix</b><br>
a matrix whose elements are arranged in a single row.<br>
<b>Scalar matrix</b><br>
a diagonal matrix with equal-valued elements along the<br>
diagonal.<br>
<b>Sequence</b><br>
a set of numbers, or algebraic expressions, which can be<br>
obtained from a proceeding one by a definite rule.<br>
<b>Series</b><br>
an expression showing the sum of terms in a sequence by<br>
linking the terms of the sequence with sign of addition.<br>
<b>Simple statement</b><br>
a declarative sentence that is either true or false.<br>
<b>Spreadsheet</b><br>
an interactive computer application program for<br>
organization, analysis, and storage of data.<br>
<b>Square matrix</b><br>
a matrix having the same number of rows and columns.<br>
<b>Tangent to a circle</b><br>
a straight line which touches the circle at only one point.<br>
<b>Trigonometric identities</b><br>
equations that are true for every value of variables<br>
occurring on both sides of an equation.<br>
<b>Trigonometric ratios</b><br>
are values of all trigonometric functions based on the<br>
value of the ratio of sides in a right-angled triangle.<br>
<b>Trigonometric substitution</b><br>
the substitution of trigonometric functions for other<br>
expressions.<br>
<b>Truth table</b><br>
a table that shows the validity of a compound statement<br>
depending on the truth values of its simple statements.<br>
<b>Union sets</b><br>
a set containing all the elements available in the individual<br>
sets under consideration.<br>
<b>Universal set</b><br>
a set containing all elements or members of all related<br>
sets without any repetition of elements.<br>
<b>Valid argument</b><br>
an argument┬Ā that has all true premises and a true<br>
conclusion.<br>
<b>Venn diagram</b><br>
a┬Ādiagram that uses circles or ovals to show relationship<br>
between sets and their elements.<br>
